Event title: #FutureOfNewsNJ Twitter chat with Free Press
About: Join the conversation by tweeting with the hashtag #FutureOfNewsNJ

Event title: ONA New Jersey data journalism training
Location: 485 Route 1 S Woodbridge Corporate Plaza, Bldg. E, Suite 300, Iselin, NJ
About:
Look, a lot of us got into journalism in the first place to get away from numbers – but numbers shouldn’t be feared! Data journalism skills can help you be a better and more powerful storyteller, and today, data is everywhere.

The NJ Advance Media data team will teach you how to make your life easier with Microsoft Excel, and get you started building interactive charts and maps that utilize tools that require no coding experience. Hooray!

The training will run 6-8pm, but we'll open the space at 5pm for those of you who want to arrive early, have some snacks and chat. Note that we are ONLY providing light refreshments -- snacks and drinks -- not dinner. Feel free to grab a sandwich on the way or bring your dinner along! We also know traffic on the Parkway is killer this time of day, so plan accordingly.

There is tons of parking outside NJ Advance Media's offices in Iselin. After you park, come on in to the building and we'll have someone in the lobby to direct you.

See you there!

Event title: Creative Paterson: Call to collaboration
About:
This 2-Day Meeting is NOT your typical conference. You create the agenda and lead breakout session on topics you care about; you contribute your best ideas; you collaborate with others in your community. Registration is Free & Required. Together, we will tackle the Creative Paterson Call to Collaboration Central Question:

"It's Paterson's time! How do we inspire Paterson to connect and empower all of our people, reinvigorate our economy, foster healthy neighborhoods, celebrate our achievements and become a model city for hope and opportunity?"

Put your creative thinking caps on and join a diverse group of people from throughout the City! This gathering includes residents, business owners, seniors, educators, students and community, faith-based, nonprofit, and municipal leaders – all who have a stake in the future of Paterson!

Parking and directions information will be emailed prior to the event. Join the Creative Paterson Facebook Group to start the conversation now: https://www.facebook.com/groups/CreativePaterson/

For questions, please contact Creative Paterson Team member Kacy O'Brien: kobrien@creativenj.org.

Event title: Engaging with Your Community: A strategy workshop for news publishers
About:
Your community doesn't want just to read what you write.  It wants to connect. It wants to be heard. It wants to let you know what issues and concerns are top of mind.

It wants you to help the community tell its own stories, in multiple ways.     Doing all this will help make your site more vibrant, connected, trusted and sustainable. But it can also seem daunting, particularly when you barely have enough hours in the day already. Still, the rewards can be vast, if you can master techniques – some simple, some sophisticated – for enlisting community members as your advisers, scouts, co-producers and chorus. 

We know you probably already know all this; news publishers have been bombarded with this message over the last several years. But, do you have a plan for how to actually tackle community engagement in a meaningful way that actually makes a difference? In this workshop, which is geared toward news organizations, veteran journalist and civic engagement expert Chris Satullo will share some theory and proven tricks, then help you workshop your own civic engagement strategy. Satullo is co-director of the Pennsylvania Project for Civic Engagement, former VP for news and civic dialogue at WHYY public media and former editorial page editor of the Philadelphia Inquirer. 

This four-hour session will include lunch. Space is limited to the first 25 registrants. Tickets are free for NJ News Commons members, thanks to support from the Geraldine R. Dodge Foundation, Democracy Fund and the Community Foundation of New Jersey. 

Free parking is available and will be sent to partipants after registratoin. Note that the building is one block from the NJ Patco station and three blocks from NJ Riverline. 

If you are not a member of the NJ News Commons, please visit this site to read more and sign up.

Event title: Collaborative and Engaged Journalism Workshop in Detroit
Location: MSU Detroit Center, 3408 Woodward Ave, Multi Purpose Room, Detroit, MI 48201
About:
Detroit is a city with a robust and award-winning journalism ecosystem that serves as a national model of innovation, collaboration, and engagement. Legacy and commercial media remain prominent in reach and distribution while emerging and new media engagement with citizens is steadily growing. But based on recent reports and our explorations, there remain opportunities to:

deepen collaboration and grow mutually beneficial partnerships between commercial, legacy, non-profit, and emerging media,

empower community members to participate in news media creation and build trust with news organizations, and

provide opportunities for local citizens to be heard and see themselves reflected in the news.

In this ethos of collaboration, University of Oregon School of Journalism and Communication’s Agora Journalism Center is partnering with the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University to make space for Detroit-area news leaders, civic and media entrepreneurs, and local thought leaders not merely to talk but to identify concrete steps for meaningful outcomes. We recognize how precious your time is and do not wish to host yet another time-consuming gathering as we are all feeling that meeting fatigue. But by using tried and true facilitation techniques, our hope is to surface collaborative and engagement approaches to answer the needs listed above and to amplify and accelerate current work, in order to support a thriving information ecosystem in Detroit.

You’ve been identified as someone who can meaningfully contribute to the conversation and workshop. I invite you to our upcoming workshop, taking place at MSU Detroit Center on July 30-31, 2018. (We’ll get together in the early afternoon on Monday and have programming through the day on Tuesday, ending at 5 p.m.).

Thanks to grants from the John S. and James L. Knight Foundation and The Democracy Fund, the workshop is free of charge. Space is limited. If you are interested in attending, please register by June 29 at which time we’ll extend the invitation to others.

Please feel free to email us with any questions.

Event title: Using OPRAmachine to file public records requests without the hassle
About:
"Opening up New Jersey government, one request at a time." That's the tagline of OPRAmachine.com, a free tool created by New Jersey's own Gavin Rozzi and available for all to use.

Every citizen has the right to access public records held by state & local government agencies under the New Jersey Open Public Records Act (OPRA). By law, they have to respond. The problem is, the process for requesting those documents isn't always as easy and straightforward as it should be – until now.

Join this webinar and Rozzi will show you the simple, step-by-step process for filing OPRA requests and browsing OPRAmachine to see what public records and information others have requested.

Date: August 21, 2018
Time: 2pm-3pm
Cost: Free

Event title: A User's Guide to Democracy
Location: They send you eight emails (one per week) until Election Day.
About:
"Eight personalized emails to teach you how to make a difference"

Welcome to the User’s Guide to Democracy. We sat down with experts on Congress, voting, elections, and political campaigns, and asked all our questions about how things really work. Then we got rid of the jargon, and used what we learned to create an eight-step program to make you a more informed, more engaged, more confident voter.

Starting in September, we’ll be emailing you about once a week. Here’s what you can expect:

• What you need to know about how to vote where you live
• Personalized info on the candidates and campaigns in your Congressional district
• Tools and tactics for understanding political advertising and money in politics
• Specific insights into your Congress members' priorities and interests
• Customized voting reminders for election day and early voting dates
• “Action items” to help you put your new knowledge to work for the issues you care about

Event title: Newsletter fundamentals webinar (NJ News Commons only)
About:
Newsletters are a critical distribution tool for news publishers, but growing the subscriber list while keeping open rates high can be a challenge. Join this webinar to learn the fundamentals of email list growth using organic and paid approaches, as well as the latest best practices for creating engaging email campaigns and staying out of the spam box.

Use this EventBrite to sign up for a Newsletter Fundamentals webinar, which will be done via video conference in small groups with consultant Ned Berke.

This webinar will cover:

Tactics to grow your list organically

Subject lines and formats that drive open and click rates

Creative engagement strategies and formats to build loyalty

The basics of a FB marketing campaigns for email list growth

Avoiding the spam box

Since we'll be doing the webinars in small groups of 6 people or less, we will have plenty of time to answer your questions during this 90-minute seminar.

After you sign up, we'll send you information on how to access the webinar, and a note about the things we will need you to do in advance.

If you have any questions, feel free to contact Stefanie Murray at the Center for Cooperative Media at murrayst@montclair.edu.

Newsletter Fundamentals is the most recent addition to the Audience Development Fundamentals webinar series coordinated by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University under a grant from the Knight Foundation. More than 160 members from the Institute for Nonprofit News and the Local Independent Online News Publishers organizations have benefited from these webinars, which also include Facebook and Google/SEO Fundamentals. More dates and topics are being added through the end of 2018, and you can see the current lineup on our Eventbrite profile.

What past participants are saying about presenter Ned Berke:

“Ned helped us add subscribers to our email newsletter by giving is a whole list of improvements that we could easily make - he also gave us ideas for how to improve it in the future. He helped us prioritize our business plan and more than anything, it was just great to talk through our ideas with someone who has done this and been through it and really knows what he is talking about.”

“Ned helped me get see big picture for audience development across multiple platforms and formulate a list of steps we need to take. Now we can prioritize. Do the easy stuff quick and strategically tackle the more involved efforts.”

“The session was incredibly helpful for me because I know there are a million things we could be doing better/more efficiently but Ned helped me prioritize what we could do with the manpower and limitations that we currently have.”

“Ned Berke does an excellent job helping people like myself who have less technical backgrounds understand what we need to succeed online.”

Event title: Workshop: Collaborative journalism for local journos (plus printing press tour!)
About: This meet up will be all about COLLABORATIVE JOURNALISM! Collaboration and partnership are all the rage right now in our industry, and for good reason: News organizations are finding that working together on certain projects can greatly enhance their journalism, produce more impact and widen their reach. We'll talk about what collaboration means, who is doing it, and how local journalists can work with national newsrooms on collaborative projects. This training event will include a presentation by Stefanie Murray from the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University and a workshop with Ziva Branstetter, an editor with Reveal from the Center for Investigative Reporting. We'll start with a light breakfast (coffee, tea, bagels, fruit) at 9 a.m. The training session and workshop will run from 9:30 a.m. to 11:30 a.m. Then at 11:30 a.m., we'll break for a tour of North Jersey Media Group's printing facility. Note that North Jersey traffic is unpredictable, so if you miss breakfast, no worries. You can grab a bagel while we're workshopping! See you there!

Event title: How you can use NJ.com's police use of force dataset
Featured image: Screenshot of The Force Report. YouTube. (https://v5.airtableusercontent.com/v3/u/51/51/1774058400000/JHwLORVjpRM8DGXD3Ki3WQ/64TkWfYsAffN9rI-g84XCZhEBVKzgqNutch-gT5u1lVcyyHIVQEE7XeDia4KOLS2BPyJV921gG6H2N8OMmoXpDzPhDyulFdsmZxV6-LNX086HQXFnYa3KYYF3VkQ0T60HKP6C2aIS-sVY5gBWpHNqw/v9GNGyoUZGRrS_i_csJ2jO7NFf7Ql6UA_GG5iiytUKs)
About:
NJ.com is dropping a big investigation on Nov. 29, and they're publishing all of their data along with it. There are local stories for months in this database and we can't get to all of them.

-----

The greatest authority granted police officers is the right to punch, kick, pepper spray or use some other force against another person. Two decades ago, state officials envisioned a centralized database that would flag overly aggressive departments or officers for scrutiny and intervention, helping prevent unnecessary injuries and costly excessive force lawsuits. But that database was never created. So we built it.

Beginning [on Nov. 29], search the most comprehensive statewide database of police force in the United States. Your town, your local department and your local officers. This data has never before been available to anyone in the state, and we've put it all into one, easy to use and searchable database for the public.

Check out our preview at nj.com/force.

-----

Steve Stirling will lead this webinar and talk with you about how best to use this data in your reporting. He will also answer any questions about the dataset. 

More about The Force Report:
https://youtu.be/q-cTjjLRDZI
https://projects.nj.com/investigations/force-trailer/

-----

Event title: Webinar: Help Reveal investigate abusive rehab centers in New Jersey
About:
The Center for Cooperative Media will host an informational webinar about the Rehab Reporting Network on Jan. 23, 2019 at 1pm ET with Byard Duncan of Reveal. During this session, Byard will talk more about the Rehab Reporting Network and how to join, and will give everyone a quick rundown of the tips we have now.

Here is a rough outline of what will be covered during the webinar:

• Intro to the topic of work-based rehabs
• What we've found in our reporting so far (Oklahoma, NC, etc.)
• Why we decided to start the network (We had so many tips coming in)
• The tips we have in NJ, and how to get more (i.e. callouts in NJ partners' own newsletters and on social)
• How to join the network and what you get from us: Access to tips, Slack channel, reporting tips, etc.

Location: School of Communication and Media building, Room 2109, Montclair State University, 1 Normal Avenue, Montclair, NJ 07043
About: Join us for an introductory workshop where you'll get hands-on experience shooting 360° photos and videos to enhance your reporting and give your community a more immersive experience! Matt MacVey of the Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at CUNY will lead this workshop. More details TK!

Event title: Introducing a new toolkit for newsletters from the Lenfest Institute
Location: School of Communication and Media building, Room 2109, Montclair State University, 1 Normal Avenue, Montclair, NJ 07043
About: Joseph Lichterman will walk us through the ins and outs of Lenfest’s latest newsletter toolkit for local publishers. This workshop will give our partners an inside look at the latest in newsletter knowledge sharing and lessons learned, and is based on more than a year of work by Lichterman and the rest of the Lenfest Institute. Food and refreshments will be provided.

Event title: Library research tools and tips for New Jersey journalists
About:
New Jersey's libraries are rich resources for journalists, but we often forget -- or don't realize -- the depth of information that's available.

This webinar, hosted by the New Jersey State Library Information Center, will aim to educate journalists on some of the most overlooked information and tools available to the public that could be useful for community storytelling.

The webinar will cover topics including:

Finding and accessing publicly-available New Jersey documents, including the library’s significant digital collections and resources beyond the library’s collection.
Legal research tools.
Research guides developed by library staff to assist users in discovering resources.
U.S. documents and federal resources.

The presenters include staff from the New Jersey State Library Information Center:

• Maria Baratta, Associate Director
• Deborah Mercer, New Jersey Collections Librarian
• Teri Taylor, U.S. Documents Librarian
• Cynthia Lambert, Law Librarian

The presentation will run about 50 minutes, with 10 minutes left for questions at the end.

To register for this library research tools and tips event, visit the link below:

https://attendee.gotowebinar.com/register/3341797765141841410 

After registering, you will receive a confirmation email containing information about joining the webinar.

If you have any questions, email the Center for Cooperative Media at info@centerforcooperativemedia.org.

Event title: Share + learn about local collaboratives with Resolve Philadelphia
Location: Comcast Technology Center, 1800 Arch Street, Philadelphia, PA 19103
About:
Over the past year, newsrooms throughout the U.S. have shown an increasing interest in forming collaboratives among local counterparts to deepen the impact and reach of their own reporting. Many have looked to Resolve Philadelphia for guidance, because of its pioneering efforts leading the award-winning initiatives the Reentry Project and Broke in Philly.

On May 16, 2019, Resolve Philadelphia and the Solutions Journalism Network cordially invite those passionate about local collaboration to join them for peer-learning gathering from 10 a.m. to 3:30 p.m. in Philadelphia. This event will be a pre-convening to the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University's annual Collaborative Journalism Summit, which begins on the evening of the 16th in Philly.

What this pre-convening isn’t:

A gathering for people who are still questioning the value of collaboration in journalism. We will spend time forming relationships and discussing best practices, not convincing folks about why this work makes sense

What this pre-convening is:

• A chance for those who are passionate about local collaboration-- especially journalists who are in the process of, or have already formed local newsroom collaborations--to meet one another, share experiences and build connections to be long-term thought-partners

• A gathering to talk through the nitty-gritty of local newsroom collaboration: strategies for solving common pain points, the best tools for communication, and more

• An opportunity to speak with representatives from the 20+ Broke in Philly and Reentry Project partners on their successes and challenges over the past two years.

• A chance to hear from Resolve Philadelphia leadership on the results of their Impact Evaluation of the Reentry Project, and the development of their new Impact tracking system, specially designed for collaborative solutions journalism projects

• An opportunity to talk with representatives from the Solutions Journalism Network about how a reporting project with a solutions lens can help change the community conversation and overcome traditional barriers among news professionals.

The agenda will be based on input from attendees gathered through an online survey, and the day will include opportunities for both formal and informal learning.

There is no fee to attend. The Center for Cooperative Media is generously making available limited travel stipends for journalists who would otherwise not be able to attend this pre-convening or the Summit through their Peer Learning and Collaborations Fund. We strongly encourage newsrooms who have the funds to cover their staff’s travel costs.

Event title: 2019 Collaborative Journalism Summit
Location: WHYY Studios, 150 N. Sixth Street, Philadelphia, PA 19106
About:
You're invited
We hope you'll join us and other journalists, media executives, technologists and entrepreneurs from around the world for the 2019 Collaborative Journalism Summit.

This year's conference builds on the successes of our 2017 and 2018 summits, which focused on case studies of successful collaborative projects and featured keynote speakers representing CrossCheck, ethnic media collaborations, The Panama Papers, Solutions Journalism Network, Electionland and much more.

The 2019 Collaborative Journalism Summit is set for May 16-17 at the School of Communication and Media on the campus of Montclair State University in Montclair, N.J.

Space is limited to the first 180 registrants, so reserve your spot today. Early-bird reservations are $50 per ticket. The ticket will include two nights of appetizers and cocktails, breakfast, lunch, snacks and T-shirt or conference tote bag.

We'll collecting ideas for speakers and workshops now, so if you've got one to pitch, use this form to tell us about it. We will begin to announce speakers in early 2019.

If you are interested in sponsoring the conference, visit this page to see sponsorship opportunities.

In the meantime, you can also subscribe to our collaborative journalism newsletter for more updates and information.

Have any questions? Let us know! Email conference organizer Stefanie Murray at murrayst@montclair.edu. 

Hope to see you on May 16!



TRANSPORTATION DETAILS

If you are driving: The summit will be held in the School of Communication and Media at Montclair State University. Navigate to the Red Hawk Parking Deck on the campus of Montclair State University. We'll give you a pass so parking will be free. From there you can walk to the School of Communication and Media; exit out of the parking deck on the 4th floor and walk straight ahead on to campus; you'll see construction directly in front of you. Veer along the walking path to the left of the construction and follow the path that hugs the construction fence straight back. The School of Communication and Media Building will be ahead and to the left at the end of the path.

If you take the train: On NJ Transit, take the Montclair-Boonton Line and get off at Montclair Heights, NOT at the Montclair State University stop. (There are several stops in Montclair.) Coming off the train, you will make a right onto Normal Avenue and cross the street, walking on the edge of campus. Make a left on College Avenue and walk on to campus. You'll pass our Red Hawk statue and College Hall, which is under construction, on your right. After you walk past College Hall, follow the walking path that veers right and hugs the construction fence; follow that path straight back and the School of Communication and Media Building will be ahead and to the left at the end of the path.

If you are flying in: Newark Liberty International Airport (EWR) is the closest airport. If flying in to Newark doesn't work for you, you could also fly in to NYC and drive or take public transit in to New Jersey.

Ridesharing: If you plan to use a ride-sharing service to get to the summit, we have set up a promo code with Lyft; use the code 2019COLLABJ for $5 off your ride to or from the conference. Be sure to have your driver navigate to the Red Hawk Parking Deck; that will ensure you get dropped off at the right place on campus.

HOTEL

There are many hotel options in the Montclair area, and of course many more in NYC if you plan to stay there. We will arrange for a group rate at a local hotel; check back in early 2019 for more details.

Event title: State of Ethnic Media in New Jersey: Research report + luncheon
Location: Paterson Free Public Library, 250 Broadway, Paterson, NJ 07501
About:
The Center will release its latest research report, "State of Ethnic Media in New Jersey," and hold a discussion about the findings.
About this Event
Ethnic and community media are a critical, yet poorly understood, part of the news and information ecosystem. The Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University has been studying this sector of the media industry for the last several months and will release its report "State of Ethnic Media" on June 3.

Join us that day for lunch and a presentation about the report by authors Dr. Sarah Stonbely, Ph.D., the research director of the Center for Cooperative Media, and Anthony Advincula, a New York City-based journalist, media consultant and former national media director for New America Media. The presentation will be followed by a roundtable discussion about the report's findings and recommendations.

This event is free thanks to operational support for the Center from the Geraldine R. Dodge Foundation.

Through a combination of database work, interviews, and surveys, this report provides a comprehensive list of ethnic and community media serving New Jersey, transmits thoughts from publishers and established reporters about contemporary struggles and triumphs at these outlets, and documents the transition to digital, as well as their outlooks for the year ahead.

Event title: Census workshop for New Jersey media
Location: Rutgers University, Newark campus, Paul Robeson Campus Center, 350 Dr. MLK Jr. Blvd  Essex Room, Newark, NJ 07102
About:
The U.S. Census is a monumental task that underpins the workings of our nation's democracy, and the results of the upcoming Census will deeply affect communities for years to come.

That's why it is critical for media to play a role in getting accurate and timely information about the Census out to their audiences.

But covering the Census can be confusing and the amount of data overwhelming.

Join us on Aug. 22 for a workshop about the Census tailored specifically for New Jersey media and individual journalists. The workshop will include:

An overview of the Census and the local response, led by the chair of the NJ Census 2020 committee.
An overview of the Census Public Information Office and the resources available for journalists, the communications program planned around the 2020 Census and a timeline of what we will see during the next year.
An overview of local partnership and activities from the New York regional office.
An tutorial on how to use the Census platform and data tools.
A conversation with New Jersey journalists who have already been covering the Census about their approach, challenges they've faced and what they are expecting.
A conversation with nonprofits and community organizers leading the response across New Jersey.
Morning refreshments and lunch will be provided. The workshop is being coordinated by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University and is funded by the Geraldine R. Dodge Foundation. A limited number of travel stipends will be made available if traveling to, or participating in, the workshop creates an economic hardship.

Event title: Share + learn about service journalism with Outlier Media
Location: TechTown Detroit, 440 Burroughs Street, Detroit, MI 48202
About:
Outlier Media's founder Sarah Alvarez knew there were thousands of residents in Detroit whom no media was serving. The kind of news being produced by traditional news organizations was not useful, not relevant, not valuable.

Outlier Media was born as a service journalism organization dedicated to giving residents the high-value information they needed. Through deep community information needs assessments, Alvarez found that housing and utilities were the areas where Detroiters had the highest information gaps. Outlier delivers information daily via text message to residents to close the information gaps that persist around housing and utilities.

Join us on Sept. 27 for a day of sharing and learning with Outlier Media. This event is hosted by Outlier Media and the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University through the Peer Learning + Collaboration Fund.

The day will include a deep-dive into how Outlier Media works, open discussion with its founder and team, and a reporting bus tour to show attendees who Outlier is serving. Lunch will be provided.

There is no fee to attend, although a $25 deposit will be required as space is limited and some folks will be traveling from quite a ways to make the trip -- we can't have anyone taking a seat who won't show up. The deposit will be refunded the day after the event.

The Center for Cooperative Media is making available travel stipends for journalists who would otherwise not be able to attend this convening through the Peer Learning + Collaboration Fund. The deposit will be waived for Peer Fund award winners. Apply ASAP so you can get your grant before the trip! We strongly encourage newsrooms who have the funds to cover their staff’s travel costs.

Note that if you're applying for a travel award through the Peer Fund, you must do that AND reserve your spot via this EventBrite page. If you have any questions, contact Stefanie Murray at murrayst@montclair.edu.

Event title: Media & immigration: How the current political & social climate affects coverage
Location: Reformed Church of Highland Park, 19 S 2nd Ave, Highland Park, NJ 08904, USA
About:
Reporting on immigration in a time of intense divisiveness and heated political rhetoric can be challenging for local journalists. And while many newsrooms provide quality coverage, there’s been a fair share of coverage that dehumanizes immigrants, does not meet their needs, or worse, amplifies misleading or hateful rhetoric.

It’s vital for the public that newsrooms get this local and national story right. Join a conversation on Oct. 22 with immigration reporters and experts in our state about media coverage of immigration, what it's been like to cover the topic in the current political and social climate, and how it can better center community perspectives and tell authentic stories with impact.

The discussion will be moderated by Juan D. González, an award-winning broadcast journalist and investigative reporter who is co-host of Democracy Now. González is a professor of communications and public policy at Rutgers University. He is also the author of "Harvest of Empire: A History of Latinos in America," and a founder of the National Association of Hispanic Journalists.

González will be joined by Monsy Alvarado, who reports on immigration and diversity for NorthJersey.com, part of the USA Today Network; Matt Katz, who reports for WNYC about immigration, refugees, national security and hate; and Hera Mir, who oversees communications and operations for the New Jersey Alliance for Immigrant Justice.

At least one additional reporter will be added to the panel as they are confirmed closer to the date.

Seating is limited to 50. The event will open with a networking hour from 5-6 p.m. with light refreshments, and the discussion will run 6-7:30 p.m.

Event title: Google Analytics Fundamentals: Using data to drive engaged audiences
About:
Google Analytics is one of the most powerful free tools available to publishers, allowing them to measure their success growing and engaging audiences. Join this webinar to learn the fundamentals of using Google Analytics with a special focus on metrics that reflect engagement, and prioritizing dashboards that help you establish strategies to drive ongoing, loyal readership. 
The webinar will also feature discussion of the new Audience Explorer tool.
Use this EventBrite to sign up for a Google Analytics Fundamentals webinar, which will be done via video conference in small groups with consultant Ned Berke.
This webinar will cover:


Explanations of the most common metrics, and what they mean for publishers


Key metrics that reflect engagement


The three most important reports for publishers, and how to use them to measure success


The audience funnel theory of reader relationships, and how to track user's progress through that funnel in Google Analytics


Identifying your most valuable segments, and surfacing critical insights about their behavior


An introduction to more advanced Google Analytics features, including conversion goals and custom dimensions


This webinar is oriented to those looking to make the most of a standard, out-of-the-box Google Analytics installation.
Since we'll be doing the webinars in small groups of 10 people or less, we will have plenty of time to answer your questions during this 90-minute seminar.
After you sign up, we'll send you information on how to access the webinar, and a note about the things we will need you to do in advance.
If you have any questions, feel free to contact Stefanie Murray at the Center for Cooperative Media at murrayst@montclair.edu.
Google Analytics Fundamentals is the most recent addition to the Audience Development Fundamentals webinar series coordinated by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University under grants from the Knight Foundation and Facebook. More than 250 individuals from dozens of news organizations in the Institute for Nonprofit News, the Local Independent Online News Publishers, the Detroit Journalism Cooperative and the NJ News Commons organizations have benefited from these webinars, which also include Newsletter, Facebook and Google/SEO Fundamentals. You can see the current lineup on our Eventbrite profile.

Event title: Working toward a healthy, collaborative news ecosystem in Newark
Location: 323 Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Blvd Central King Building Room 116, Newark, NJ 07102
About:
Join your peers working in Newark  media to learn about healthy, collaborative news ecosystems and how you can create one.
 As traditional models underpinning the news industry have collapsed, it's become clear that establishing and supporting connected, healthy and collaborative news ecosystems will be a critical way to respond to the information needs of communities. 
Innovations are underway in places such as Chicago, Detroit, Philadelphia and Denver. Join us on NJIT's campus for a daylong workshop on how Newark can build such a renewed news ecosystem.
The workshop will include an interactive discussion about healthy news ecosystems and a reflective deep-dive into your role in the region's information environment and how working together could improve it. You'll walk away with an understanding of how to work toward a collaborative news ecosystem in Newark, ideas you can start working on immediately, resources you'll need and your role in it all.  A diverse range of journalists, mediamakers and funders who are from and serve Newark will be in attendance. 
Lunch and refreshments will be provided. Small stipends will also be made available for those participants for whom attending the full-day workshop creates an economic hardship.
The invite-only workshop is presented by the Peer Learning + Collaboration Fund, a project of the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University that is generously supported by Democracy Fund. 
The workshop will be facilitated in part by Fiona Morgan of Branchhead Consulting and Stefanie Murray of the Center for Cooperative Media.
Please note that the workshop will be conducted under Chatham House Rule.
For those driving to the event, parking will be provided in the parking deck at 154 Summit St.
Last, we encourage workshop attendees to consider staying on campus at NJIT in the evening after our event to attend Creative  New Jersey's Newark Call to Collaboration. The Call to Collaboration gathering is intended to bring together Newark residents, business owners, youth, nonprofit, education, social service and faith-based leaders, government personnel and others for an "ideas-to-action" conference on ways  to strengthen the city. Click here to read more and register. 
Any questions, comments, suggestions? Let the Peer Fund organizers know: peerfund@collaborativejournalism.org.

Event title: AMA: Steve Stirling answers your questions about reporting on COVID-19

Event title: Google Analytics Fundamentals: Using data to drive engaged audiences
About:
Google Analytics is one of the most powerful free tools available to publishers, allowing them to measure their success growing and engaging audiences. Explanations of the most common metrics, and what they mean for publishers

The three most important reports for publishers, and how to use them to measure success

Event title: Share + learn about local news co-ops with The Devil Strip
Location: 12 E Exchange St, Akron, OH 44308, USA
About:
The Devil Strip has become a leader in independent, mission-oriented news, thanks in part to how it’s applying a multi-stakeholder cooperative ownership model to local journalism.

Join us April 9, 2020 for a day of sharing and learning with The Devil Strip staff, contributors, and some of the nearly 400 community members who’ve become co-owners. This event is hosted by The Devil Strip and the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University through the Peer Learning + Collaboration Fund. Support is also being provided by the Community Information Cooperative.

The day will include a deep-dive into how The Devil Strip got started, why it chose community-ownership and how a cooperatively-owned newsroom works, both operationally and editorially. You’ll meet some of the nearly 200 Akronites who’ve contributed as writers, editors, photographers, and illustrators to help shape the civic narrative of Akron through The Devil Strip, as well as some of the member-owners who now share the responsibility of telling the city’s story and meeting the community’s information needs.

Event title: A telebriefing on the coronavirus crisis with NJ public health experts
About:
Join us for a telebriefing with NJ public health experts on April 17.
As the novel coronavirus continues to affect our lives and communities, the Center for Cooperative Media will host a press call Friday, April 17 at 2:00 p.m. to discuss the latest updates on the NJ state response to the COVID-19 pandemic. 
Montclair University experts will cover topics such as:
- The outlook and public health response in New Jersey
- Barriers and challenges for NJ ethnic and immigrant communities
- Next steps and policies in NJ
WHAT
The Center for Cooperative Media will provide an update to media on the COVID-19 response in New Jersey
WHO
- Dr. Stephanie Silvera is a professor of public health at Montclair State University. She is an expert in epidemiology and cancer epidemiology, and her scholarship focuses on the racial and ethnic disparities in cancer outcomes.
- Dr. Sze Yan Liu is an assistant professor at Montclair State University. She joined the Department of Public Health from her position as a research scientist at the New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene. Her scholarship focuses on the impact of social policies on health disparities and how social factors affect healthcare utilization. She speaks conversational Cantonese.
WHEN
Friday, April 17, 2020 at 2 pm ET.

Event title: Crowdfunding Fundamentals with Kickstarter
About:
Learn how crowdfunding can help support local news
Crowdfunding can unlock vital resources to support local news projects, building community and recruiting readers to be passionate backers of journalism. In times of crisis, when other revenue streams are stretched thin, it can ensure budgets dedicated to specific lines of reporting or product initiatives. But success depends on a good idea, a great pitch and a framework for effectively engaging potential supporters throughout the process. 
Join Kickstarter’s Senior Journalism Outreach Lead Oriana Leckert to learn about crowdfunding strategies that can work on any platform, and go behind-the-scenes of prior Kickstarter campaigns that have raised thousands of dollars to support news reporting efforts. The session will be followed by a Q&A facilitated by Center for Cooperative Media’s Ned Berke.
Use this EventBrite to sign up for our Crowdfunding Fundamentals w/Kickstarter webinar, which will be done via video conference. Webinar attendees will be invited to work with Oriana after the call to develop their own projects to potentially be featured on Kickstarter. 

This webinar will cover:
- An overview of Kickstarter and Kickstarter Journalism
- A primer on crowdfunding during a pandemic
- Representative case studies of different types of successful KSR Journalism campaigns Launching a new endeavor
- Funding a specific beat or topic
- Leveling up an organization
- Creating a special projectDesigning something episodic
- Elements of an effective campaign, across any crowdfunding platform
- Creative prompts Kickstarter is currently promoting 
- Q&A

After you sign up, we'll send you information on how to access the webinar. 
Oriana Leckert is an arts & culture writer and the Senior Journalism Outreach Lead at Kickstarter. She’s written and edited for a variety of outlets, including Vice, MTV News, Slate, Hyperallergic, Gothamist, Atlas Obscura, Pitchfork, and The Believer.
Ned Berke works with local, regional, niche and investigative news organizations to strengthen relationships with audiences to support quality journalism. More than 300 small and medium publishers have received strategic assistance through his work with Center for Cooperative Media. 
If you have any questions, feel free to contact Stefanie Murray at the Center for Cooperative Media at murrayst@montclair.edu.

Event title: How New Jersey reporters are covering the current coronavirus pandemic
About:
Join two veteran New Jersey reporters to discuss how you can improve your reporting about COVID-19 in our state
The lives of thousands of immigrants and people of color in New Jersey have been disproportionately upended by this unprecedented health crisis. With unemployment breaking records, churches facing a heavy financial toll, parents falling behind in rent and struggling to make ends meet, and food bank lines stretching for miles — there are critical stories in these already vulnerable communities that need to be told.
In this webinar, you’ll learn what are the issues in this deepening crisis have not been reported, must be reported and how should it be reported. You'll also explore finding the right sources, looking at fresh angles in your community and getting factual updates on where the state is with COVID-19. 
This webinar is being produced thanks to support from the Geraldine R. Dodge Foundation and Democracy Fund.
About the speakers
The webinar will feature the following speakers:
Lindy Washburn, who is the healthcare reporter for the USATODAY Network’s five newspapers/websites in New Jersey and a contributor to Gannett’s regional health coverage. Based at NorthJersey.com and The Record, she has covered healthcare in New Jersey for 30 years and received many awards. Before joining The Record, she reported for the Associated Press in New York, Hartford, and Honolulu. And before that, she spent five years doing rural development work in Muslim areas of the southern Philippines. She majored in Chinese/East Asian studies in college and has a master’s degree from the Columbia University Graduate School of Journalism. 
John Mooney, who co-founded NJ Spotlight in 2009 as an independent news and information source focusing on public policy and politics in New Jersey.  Before NJ Spotlight, John covered education in New Jersey for 15 years as a reporter for The Newark Star-Ledger and The Bergen Record and as a contributing writer for The New York Times. He continues to be a regular guest on New Jersey public media, including WHYY and WNYC public radio, and last year, NJ Spotlight was acquired by WNET to be a partner of its public TV newscast, NJTV News. He and NJ Spotlight have won numerous state and national awards, including honors from the Education Writers Association, the American Society of News Editors and the Online News Association.

Event title: LinkedIn Fundamentals
About:
LinkedIn isn't just for jobs. It's a place where the right kind of content can flourish. An estimated 30% of LinkedIn's audience uses the platform for news, according to the latest data from Pew Research. That number has been rising for years, creating a steady steam of traffic and audience growth for savvy publishers. LinkedIn audiences tend to be among the most loyal and engaged social audiences.

Join this webinar to learn how publishers are using LinkedIn to find new audiences and engage with readers. We'll cover editorial best practices, tactics for improving the visibility and spread of your content on the platform and what types of content perform best. We'll also talk through LinkedIn's editorial functions and ways to minimize time spent in managing publishing to LinkedIn while maximizing traffic and engagement.

This webinar is open to members of Institute for Nonprofit News, the Local Independent Online News Publishers, the Detroit Journalism Cooperative, the NJ News Commons, the Colorado Media Project and the Association of Alternative Newsmedia.

Use this EventBrite to sign up for a LinkedIn Fundamentals webinar, which will be done via video conference with consultant Meena Thiruvengadam. 

This webinar will cover:
- How to find your niche on LinkedIn
- How to work with LinkedIn
- Content that outperforms on LinkedIn
- Best practices for editorial on LinkedIn
- Engaging with LinkedIn audiences

Since we'll be doing the webinars in small groups, we will have plenty of time to answer your questions during this 90-minute seminar.
After you sign up, we'll send you information on how to access the webinar.

If you have any questions, feel free to contact Stefanie Murray at the Center for Cooperative Media at murrayst@montclair.edu.

Event title: Reddit Fundamentals
About:
Reddit is a place where content can flourish, engagement can drive traffic, and stories that dominate online conversation can be discovered.

Event title: Assessing Newark's information needs and filling the gaps
About:
Join Newark media colleagues and key community leader to learn about information needs and how we can collaboratively meet them better.
In Newark, the coronavirus pandemic has laid bare the fact that many people don't have access to the kind of information they need to live their daily lives. 
There are many reasons for these information need gaps, including the fact that the news industry has contracted so much over the last decade. 
Join Newark journalists, media makers, key community leaders and funders on Aug. 11 for a workshop to discuss information needs, Newark and how we can identify and fill gaps in the city by working collaboratively. 
The workshop will include an interactive discussion about critical information needs and the information ecosystem in Newark, as well as an invitation to participate in a city-wide information needs assessment and a follow-up grant opportunity. 
The post-workshop grant opportunity, made possible with support from The Nicholson Foundation and Victoria Foundation, will ONLY be open to workshop participants. More information will be shared during the workshop.
Stipends will be made available for those participants for whom attending the event creates an economic hardship.
This invite-only workshop is presented by the Peer Learning + Collaboration Fund, a project of the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University that is generously supported by Democracy Fund. 
The event will be hosted via Zoom conference and facilitated in part by Fiona Morgan of Branchhead Consulting and Stefanie Murray of the Center for Cooperative Media. It will be conducted under Chatham House Rule.
Any questions, comments, suggestions? Let the Peer Fund organizers know: peerfund@collaborativejournalism.org.

Event title: How Resolve Philadelphia uses Airtable
About:
Learn about how the team at Resolve Philadelphia, the nation's most innovative collaborative, uses Airtable for project management.
Collaborative work requires sophisticated project management on every level. Join Resolve Philly as we talk through our system for tracking paperwork, impact, and production. Our main tool for this, Airtable (not a sponsor — yet), provides a structure that allows us to draw connections between all the different elements of our work and present it in a simple(ish) database. 
We'll go through how we organize our goals for our staff and overall work and how we create metrics around them through impact tracking. You'll also see how we organize our relationships with individuals and organizations so that invoices and paperwork are easily accessible. You can then leverage this whole setup to build narratives around your work without doing any backtracking — because you've organized it in your database already. 
We'll go over some of our favorite ways to use and organize the information we collect and why we collect it. Then, we'll take time to walk you through a process that can help you decide what information you should track for your projects, how you could organize it and what this all looks like in practice. 
We'll also talk about the growing pains with this system, how our use has evolved over time, and some go-to strategies for problem-solving. Project management doesn't have to just be to see progress and keep yourself on task — it can be a tool for you to streamline how you share and organize information or build narratives around the work you've accomplished. 
This webinar is part of a fall 2020 series being hosted by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University. Cassie Haynes and Jean Friedman Rudovsky of Resolve will lead the session. 
Other webinars in this series include:
Introduction to collaborative journalismHow to design a journalism collaborationWorkflow and technology considerations for collaborationsQuestions? Email  the Center at info@centerforcooperativemedia.org or visit the Center's website focused on collaborative journalism, collaborativejournalism.org.

Event title: How to fight back when people discredit your political reporting
About:
In the partisan world we live in, you know you will likely receive criticisms and accusations about your political coverage.
The 2020 election is coming up. Tensions are high and accusations of partisan bias are already flying. 
Are you ready to respond? 
The Trusting News team will explain why you shouldn't ignore claims of bias or 'fake news' and how you can strategically, efficiently and effectively defend your work.
Presenters:
Joy Mayer, director, TrustingNews.orgLynn Walsh assistant director, TrustingNews.orgMore about Trusting News:
Trusting News aims to demystify trust in news and empower journalists to take responsibility for actively demonstrating credibility and earning trust. We believe a more informed electorate that knows where to turn for quality information is key to democracy. 
Newsrooms need to understand the causes of user distrust, take ownership of the problem and be empowered to prioritize earning trust in their everyday work. Any newsroom — across platforms, ownership structures and topics — that stands behind its mission and ethics should make optimizing trust part of its job. That includes:
Committing to standards of transparency and ethics.Dedicating staff time to understanding distrust.Explaining their purpose, their decision-making and their process alongside their journalism.Using every available medium and method of communication to demonstrate credibility and actively invite and respond to audience feedback and questions.IMPORTANT: This session will be held via Zoom. Once you register, you will receive a link to join the Zoom meeting. If you have any questions or issues joining the meeting, please send an email to info@centerforcooperativemedia.org.

Event title: Election SOS: Planning for post-election scenarios
About:
The next several weeks will be hard. Election SOS  has a Scenario Planning Guide to help newsrooms and journalists plan.
It's entirely possible — perhaps even inevitable — that we will see widespread protest and civil unrest in the wake of the 2020 election. Are you prepared?
Have you thought about what and how you'll report on Election Day and in the days that follow? Have you thought through how your coverage might impact people's sense of security and calm? Are you ready to cover protests? Do you have a backup plan in case you lose Internet? What if journalists on the ground in your area are arrested? What if you are arrested? What happens if someone doxxes you? How will you cover misinformation without amplifying it? What will you do if a candidate declares victory when they actually didn't win?
This is why Election SOS  exists: to support journalists with critical information needs around the 2020 election. Election SOS offers free training and resources for journalists to support election coverage. Election SOS is non-partisan and does not advocate for any policy or electoral outcome.
During this webinar, the Election SOS team will walk New Jersey newsrooms, journalists, and freelancers through its Newsroom Scenario Planning Guide  and help you prepare for a wide variety of potential election scenarios.
Click here to read the full guide.
You must register via Eventbrite in order to receive the Zoom link for this session.
Questions? Email info@centerforcooperativemedia.org.

Event title: How to create a community newsletter using basic automation
About:
Modeled after food pantries, the news harvest collects and redistributes information from dozens of sources to people across the community.
The Bloomfield Information Project is the Community Info Coop’s public service journalism lab in Bloomfield, New Jersey. It’s a town of 50,000 people that some may call a “news desert,” or place where there isn’t enough local news for and about the community.
And if you count local news articles, that’s mostly true. There are few stories with multiple sources being written by journalists reporting what’s happening in and around Bloomfield. But people and organizations are still sharing useful news and information.
The trouble is it doesn’t reach people consistently. But that’s a service local news and community organizations can provide.
Modeled after food pantries, BIP's "news harvest" collects and redistributes news and information from dozens of sources to people across the community.
During this webinar, Community Info Coop founder Simon Galperin will show how you can collect information shared by local community groups and businesses, civic organizations and individuals using a simple spreadsheet, and use that spreadsheet to automate a newsletter that aggregates it all. No coding experience is required for this session, but a basic understanding of HTML is recommended.
Learn more about the news harvest: https://www.infodistricts.org/blog/a-local-newsletter-workflow-to-improve-information-equity-in-your-community
NOTE: You MUST REGISTER VIA EVENTBRITE in order to receive the Zoom link for this session. A recording of the session will be provided to all who register.

Event title: How to design a journalism collaboration
About:
Learn how to design a successful collaborative journalism project
Every newsroom and reporter has a  process for doing journalism but it can get a little tricky to figure out what that process looks like when you're working together. In this webinar, we'll discuss the spectrum of considerations to consider internally and with partners to establish shared goals, expectations, workflow and day to day logistics.
The webinar is part of a three-month series being hosted by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University, thanks to a generous grant from Rita Allen Foundation. Heather Bryant, founder of Project Facet, will lead this design webinar.
Other webinars in this series include:
Introduction to collaborative journalismWorkflow and technology considerations for collaborationsAdditional webinars will be added when the series starts back up in June 2020.
Questions? Email  the Center at info@centerforcooperativemedia.org or visit the Center's website focused on collaborative journalism, collaborativejournalism.org.

Event title: Google Analytics Fundamentals: Using data to drive engaged audiences
About:
Google Analytics is one of the most powerful free tools available to publishers, allowing them to measure their success growing and engaging audiences. Join this webinar to learn the fundamentals of using Google Analytics with a special focus on metrics that reflect engagement, and prioritizing dashboards that help you establish strategies to drive ongoing, loyal readership. 
The webinar will also feature discussion of the new Audience Explorer tool, a set of free dashboards that allow you to view Google Analytics data through the prism of reader loyalty.
Use this EventBrite to sign up for a Google Analytics Fundamentals webinar, which will be done via video conference in small groups with consultant Ned Berke.
This webinar will cover:


Explanations of the most common metrics, and what they mean for publishers


Key metrics that reflect engagement


The three most important reports for publishers, and how to use them to measure success


The audience funnel theory of reader relationships, and how to track user's progress through that funnel in Google Analytics


Identifying your most valuable segments, and surfacing critical insights about their behavior


An introduction to more advanced Google Analytics features, including conversion goals and custom dimensions


This webinar is oriented to those looking to make the most of a standard, out-of-the-box Google Analytics installation.
There will be time for audience Q&A, so don't hesitate to bring your questions about analytics.
After you sign up, we'll send you information on how to access the webinar, and a note about the things we will need you to do in advance.
If you have any questions, feel free to contact Stefanie Murray at the Center for Cooperative Media at murrayst@montclair.edu.
Google Analytics Fundamentals is part of the Audience Development Fundamentals webinar series coordinated by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University under grants from the Knight Foundation and Facebook. Neaarly 300 individuals from dozens of news organizations in the Institute for Nonprofit News, the Local Independent Online News Publishers, the Detroit Journalism Cooperative and the NJ News Commons organizations have benefited from these webinars, which also include Newsletter, Facebook and Google/SEO Fundamentals. You can see the current lineup on our Eventbrite profile.

Event title: Newsletter Fundamentals: Tactics to grow your list + engage your audiences

Newsletters are a critical distribution tool for news publishers, but growing the subscriber list while keeping open rates high can be a challenge. Join this webinar to learn the fundamentals of email list growth using organic and paid approaches, as well as the latest best practices for creating engaging email campaigns and staying out of the spam box. 
Use this EventBrite to sign up for a Newsletter Fundamentals webinar, which will be done via video conference in small groups with consultant Ned Berke.

About:
Learn what you should consider when it comes to workflow and technology for journalism collaborations
Almost every collaboration has its own workflow and for many partners, it's matter of figuring out the common denominators of shared steps and tools and experimentation to find a workflow and toolbox that gets the job done. 
In this webinar, we'll discuss the various needs of collaborative projects: communication, content management, asset management, tasks, etc and common workflow and technology solutions to meet those needs.

Event title: Share + learn about community engagement with KPCC Los Angeles
About:
Learn how KPCC  works to deeply engage with residents across Southern CaliforniaAs more  journalists around the U.S. center local communities in their work, KPCC's engagement model has become one to learn from. 
KPCC  is part of Southern California Public Radio, a member supported public media network that operates across the region, reaching from Santa Barbara down to Los Angeles and Orange Counties and out to the Coachella Valley. It's known for its long running program Air Talk, its  LAist  podcast studio, and its community engagement programs. 
Join us on March 9 for a day of sharing and learning with KPCC. This event is hosted by Southern California Public Radio and the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University through the Peer Learning + Collaboration Fund. 
Ashley Alvarado, the award-winning director of community engagement at Southern California Public Radio, will lead the Share + Learn session. She'll explore the successes and challenges of the organization's community-centered programs.
There is no fee to attend, although registration is required to participate.
The Center for Cooperative Media is making available stipends for journalists to attend this convening through the Peer Learning + Collaboration Fund.  The Peer Fund will pay up to $500 to cover the time you'll need to invest to attend the session. Note that the Peer Fund's main focus is on supporting journalists of color, those who identify as part of a marginalized group and those who identify as having a high financial need. Click here to apply.

Event title: Info session about the NJ Civic Information Consortium grant process
About:
We will review and answer questions about the grants process for the NJ Civic Information Consortium.The New Jersey Civic Information Consortium recently announced that they are accepting grant applications for funding.
The NJ Civic Information Consortium is a first in the nation initiative to grow access to local news and information, is now accepting applications for its inaugural round of grant funding. The Consortium was created by the State of New Jersey in 2018 to grow access to civic information statewide and spark innovative media projects in communities lacking access to news and information.
The Consortium will consider applications from all parts of the state, but is especially interested in proposals that focus on better serving communities of color, immigrant communities and rural communities. 

The Consortium is focusing on three key areas for these initial grants, based on the following current pressing information needs of New Jerseyans: 

News and information that promotes community health, including information surrounding the ongoing impact of COVID-19 on our communities. 
Reporting and civic technology projects that make government information more accessible and hold public officials accountable. 
Diversifying New Jersey’s journalism pipeline, media education and journalism/storytelling training in communities that lack trusted news sources and/or where coverage is one-dimensional and doesn’t meet community needsDuring these sessions, staff members at the Center for Cooperative Media and representatives from the Consortium will go over the grant application process and answer questions from NJ residents and organizations that are interested in applying for funding.
Still have questions? Contact the Consortium via email at info@njcivicinfo.org.

Event title: Vaccine mis- and disinformation telebriefing for NJ journalists
About:
A press briefing about vaccine mis- and disinformation for New Jersey journalists.

About this Event

The Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University will host a press briefing for NJ journalists about COVID-19 vaccination misinformation and disinformation with Shaydanay Urbani of First Draft on Thursday, March 25, 2021 at 2 p.m. ET.
The discussion is open to reporters in New Jersey and will focus on misinformation especially in ethnic communities and communities of color. The session will cover how to spot mis- and disinformation about the vaccine and how to properly report on it and provide factual information to keep your community informed. The conversation will be on the record.
Speaker information:
Shaydanay Urbani is First Draft’s Partnerships and Programs Manager in New York, where she covers disinformation and trains journalists internationally in verification and responsible reporting. She has a background in criminal justice reporting, Middle Eastern languages and politics, and food policy.
Topics:
- Types of mis- and disinformation about the vaccine that are prevalent in underserved communities
- How to spot vaccine mis- and disinformation in underserved communities, what to look for, and why are these proliferating
- Poential economic impacts of mis- and disinformation, including dollars lost
- How to combat mis- and disinformation in underserved communities, including tactics and strategies
When:
Thursday, March 25, 2021 at 2 pm ET.
How to join:
Once you register, you'll receive an email with instructions on how to access the "Digital Links" section of this Eventbrite listing. That page will contain the Zoom link that we will use to host this conversation. The Center will also re-send you the Zoom link before the event.
If you need help figuring out how to access the link or how to use Zoom, please send an email to info@centerforcooperativemedia.org and someone will assist you.

Event title: Facebook Fundamentals: Step Up Your Facebook Game
About:
Learn the Facebook best practices that have stood the test of time and get answers to your questions.In this session, we’ll cover the nuts and bolts of setting up your Facebook page properly and share the best practices that have stood the test of time. This is your chance to ask the Facebook questions you’ve always been too afraid to ask and to learn how to focus your limited resources.
This session is led by Meena Thiruvengadam, whose previous jobs include leading audience development for Bloomberg and for Business Insider. At Business Insider, Meena managed a network of more than 60 Facebook pages covering a range of topics and geographies. She also launched the company's first Facebook Messenger newsbot. 
This webinar is open to members of the Institute for Nonprofit News, the Local Independent Online News Publishers, the Detroit Journalism Cooperative, the NJ News Commons, the Colorado Media Project and the Association of Alternative Newsmedia.
Facebook Fundamentals is part of the Audience Development Fundamentals webinar series coordinated by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University under grants from the Knight Foundation and Facebook.

Event title: SEO Basics for News Publishers
About:
Don't have a dedicated SEO team? Here's what you need to know.Just because you’re a team of one, doesn’t mean you can’t use SEO to your advantage. 
In this session, Meena Thiruvengadam a veteran journalist and audience developer who has worked with brands including Bloomberg, Business Insider, and Yahoo, will bring you up to speed. She’ll cover the nuts and bolts of search engine optimization, the latest developments in search, and help you think strategically about using SEO to build your brand.
This webinar is free and open to members of the Institute for Nonprofit News, the Local Independent Online News Publishers, the Detroit Journalism Cooperative, the NJ News Commons, the Colorado Media Project and the Association of Alternative Newsmedia.

Event title: Share + learn about growing membership and audience with Scalawag
About:
Learn how Scalawag has used deep community connections, events and more to diversify and grow its readership and base of supporters.As journalists across the U.S. reimagine how their work can be better connected and interwoven with communities they serve, many are turning to Scalawag for inspiration. 
Scalawag is a journalism and storytelling organization that illuminates dissent, unsettles dominant narratives, pursues justice and liberation, and stands in solidarity with marginalized people and communities in the South. 
Over the last few years, under the leadership of Cierra Hinton, Scalawag realigned its focus and moved from a small, largely volunteer-driven project to a scalable, sustainable business. The organization became a digital-first, membership-driven organization; it recently has used successful events to diversify its audience and grow membership. 
Scalawag has also focused on understanding how it could make its content more accessible for the communities it serves (Black, brown, queer and poor Southern communities), and how they can work with their audiences to take real steps toward equity and justice in the South.
Join us on April 8 for a half-day of sharing and learning with Scalawag. This event is hosted by Scalawag and the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University through the Peer Learning + Collaboration Fund.
There is no fee to attend, although registration is required to participate.
The Center for Cooperative Media is making available stipends for journalists to attend this convening through the Peer Learning + Collaboration Fund.  The Peer Fund will pay up to $500 to cover the time you'll need to invest to attend the session. Note that the Peer Fund's main focus is on supporting journalists of color, those who identify as part of a marginalized group and those who identify as having a high financial need. Click here to apply.

Event title: Social Media 20.21: Instagram, Twitter, LinkedIn, and Reddit
About:
Want to build a sustainable media outlet? You'll have to diversify and meet audiences wherever they are.This session builds on what you learned in Facebook Fundamentals or can stand on its own. Social media 20.21 covers Instagram, Twitter, LinkedIn, and Reddit, and helps news publishers develop a better idea of how to best focus limited resources. 
This session, taught by Meena Thiruvengadam —a veteran journalist who previously led audience development at Bloomberg and Business Insider — will help you better understand how the puzzle pieces fit together to help you meet your goals and why diversification is key.

Event title: 2021 Collaborative Journalism Summit
About:
Join the world's only event dedicated to the study and celebration of collaborative journalism. We invite you to join us in-place on May 19-21 for the 2021 Collaborative Journalism Summit. Please note: All  presentations are listed in the Eastern Standard Time zone.
This year's Summit will again be conducted fully online—you'll just need a device and Internet access to participate! You'll be treated to three half-days of panels, lightning talks, workshops, and discussions about a wide array of #collaborativej topics and projects. 
The 2021 Collaborative Journalism Summit will kick off  on Wednesday, May 19 and wrap up on Friday, May 21, 2021.  Sessions will be hosted each day from 1 p.m. EST until 5 p.m. EST. The event will featured three jam-packed half-days of discussions about collaborative journalism, including such participants as the International Center for Investigative Journalists, the Local Media Association and Solutions Journalism Network. 
Note that we're  not kidding when we say jam-packed; the Summit is designed to be a very fast-paced event that's full to the brim. You've been warned!
Thanks to feedback from the collaborative journalism community, we've restructured our ticket costs this year. When you register, you'll find three options—Blue, Yellow and Green. Please choose the option that best fits you and your organization's financial situation. If you're unable to pay for a ticket but want to attend the Summit, email us at summit@collaborativejournalism.org; there will be some complimentary tickets available thanks to the generosity of our sponsors. 
We'll be adding speakers and more information about the Summit to this page and the main conference schedule page in the coming weeks, so make sure to check back and keep an eye on collaborativejournalism.org/summit for more!

Thanks to our sponsors!

Social media 20.21 covers Instagram, Twitter, LinkedIn, and Reddit, and helps news publishers develop a better idea of how to best focus limited resources. 
This session, taught by Meena Thiruvengadam —a veteran journalist who previously led audience development at Bloomberg and Business Insider — will help you better understand how the puzzle pieces fit together to help you meet your goals and why diversification is key. 
This webinar is open to members of the Institute for Nonprofit News, the Local Independent Online News Publishers, the Detroit Journalism Cooperative, the NJ News Commons, the Colorado Media Project and the Association of Alternative Newsmedia.
Facebook Fundamentals is part of the Audience Development Fundamentals webinar series coordinated by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University under grants from the Knight Foundation and Facebook.

Event title: Sensitivity, accuracy and cultural nuance: Covering AAPI communities in NJ
About:
It is crucial for NJ journalists reflect on how we cove race-related issues with accuracy, sensitivity and cultural nuance.The surge of violence and verbal attacks against Asian Americans and Pacific Islanders (AAPI) has not only sparked national dialogue about anti-AAPI racism, but it has also heightened misconceptions and stereotypes of the AAPI communities in the mainstream media and culture. 
As journalists covering our communities in New Jersey, it is crucial to reflect and look closely at our responsibility in covering race-related issues with accuracy, sensitivity and cultural nuance. 
On June 16, 2o21, at 2:00 p.m. ET, join the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University for an in-depth virtual discussion on how culturally competent — values, language, experiences and beliefs — context plays a vital role in our coverage and reporting on the AAPI communities in the state.
Our panel will include Jun Choi, co-founder of Jersey Promise and former mayor of Edison, NJ; Jongwon Lee, reporter for Korean-language media and lawyer representing one of the victims' families in the Atlanta shooting; and Brian Jon, founder of Asian American Youth Council in New Jersey. The discussion will be moderated by Mary Chao, reporter for The Bergen Record.

Event title: Press event: Tracking the Delta variant in New Jersey
About:
Join us for a press briefing about the Delta variant on July 29, 2021 at 2:00 pm EST to hear and learn from Dr. David Adinaro.COVID cases are rapidly rising once again in New Jersey due to the spread of the Delta variant — almost tripling over the last few weeks. The highly contagious variant accounted for nearly 27 percent of all coronavirus strains recorded in the state as of June 19, according to the latest COVID-19 Variant Surveillance Report released by the NJ Department of Health and the CDC.
The increase in Delta has been particularly prevalent among the more than 2 million unvaccinated New Jersey residents, especially young people, driving Gov. Phil Murphy and health officials to reiterate that vaccines in the state have been extremely effective in preventing infections, hospitalizations and deaths from COVID-19.
Join us for a press briefing on July 29, 2021 at 2:00 pm EST to hear and learn from Dr. David Adinaro, deputy commissioner of public health services for the New Jersey Department of Health, about the latest updates on the Delta variant. Dr. Adinaro will discuss the latest case numbers in the state, what we need to know about the variant, Delta variant symptoms, concerns about new lockdowns, and how to keep families and communities safe.
WHO: Dr. David Adinaro, deputy commissioner, public health services — NJ Department of Health

Event title: Press event: The impacts of COVID-19 on immigrants in New Jersey
About:
Since February 2020, more than 7 million people in the U.S. have lost their jobs. People of color and immigrant families have been particularly impacted, including the 10.4 million undocumented immigrants living in the U.S. Undocumented immigrant workers lack of access to healthcare, ineligible for many government relief payments, and experience job instability. In New Jersey, there are still barriers to accessing essential services for treatment and testing of COVID-19 for immigrants.
For example, even though the USCIS announced that the “public charge” rule is no longer in effect, the chilling effects on immigrant families remain palpable; many of immigrants still avoid public programs. Without paid sick leave and proper healthcare, immigrant workers are among those who have to choose between going to work and exposing co-workers and customers to infection or going without pay for weeks.
Join the Center for Cooperative Media as we speak to Nedia Morsy of Make the Road NJ about these and other impacts that the COVID-19 pandemic has had on New Jersey's immigrant communities.
SPEAKER: Nedia Morsy, Director of Organizing, Make the Road NJ
WHEN: Friday, Sept. 17, 2021 at 1pm ET

Event title: Press briefing: The NJ eviction moratorium is coming to an end
About:
Join us on Dec. 9 for a press briefing about the end of the NJ eviction moratorium and what it means for NJ residents.

Even with the omicron variant of the coronavirus spreading across the state and many parts of the country — the eviction moratorium in New Jersey is still scheduled to come to an end on Dec. 15. NJ residents who missed rent payments due to the pandemic have until then to apply for the state’s rental aid program and be entered into a lottery to fairly distribute the remaining federal rental assistance funding.
What does this mean for low-income renters, many of whom are immigrants and people of color? What happens next, after the eviction moratorium application sunsets? Could this increase homelessness?
Join us on Thursday, Dec. 9, 2021 at 3:00 pm EST for a virtual press briefing with Renee Koubiadis, anti-poverty program director at New Jersey Citizen Action. 
The expiration of the eviction moratorium will be a big story in New Jersey in the coming days. Only a fraction of those facing eviction have filled out the paperwork that would protect them. Some tenants have even been illegally evicted during the pandemic. 
In some NJ counties, rental aid programs are already no longer taking applications. Newark’s application remains open through January 7, but Jersey City closed its application last October.

SPEAKER:
- Renee Koubiadis, anti-poverty program director at New Jersey Citizen Action
