Event title: Press briefing for NJ reporters about the Omicron variant
About:
Join us on Jan. 5 for an exclusive press briefing about the Omicron variant in New Jersey.
 
Recently, the CDC issued a warning that the Omicron variant of the coronavirus could “fuel a massive spike” in cases throughout the U.S.

The CDC predicted about 1.3 million new cases in seven days and that the death toll could shoot up 71 percent — more than 15,000 lives could be lost in a single week.

Join us on WEDNESDAY, JANUARY 5, 2022, at 2:00 pm ET, for a virtual press briefing with Dr. David Adinaro, deputy commissioner of Public Health Services at the New Jersey Department of Health, and find out the latest updates on the Omicron variant and how it is spreading across New Jersey.

How easily does Omicron spread? Will vaccines work against Omicron? Does a booster make a big difference against Omicron? What tools do we need to fight Omicron? Will treatment work against Omicron?
 
Although more than 60 percent of the U.S. population is fully vaccinated, and more than 28 percent have had booster shots, many people still don’t know much about the Omicron variant. 

New Jersey, according to the CDC, is one of the states that has detected cases of COVID-19 illness caused by the Omicron variant. 

WHAT: 
An exclusive press briefing for NJ News Commons members about the Omicron variant.
 
WHO: 
Dr. David Adinaro, deputy commissioner of Public Health Services at the NJ Department of Health
 
WHEN:
Wednesday, Jan. 5, 2022 at 2:00 pm ET
 
Questions? Contact the Center directly at info@centerforcooperativemedia.org.

Event title: Using basic Google tools for local news
About:
Join us on Jan. 26 to learn how to use basic Google tools for local news.
 
The Center for Cooperative Media is hosting a free training with Mike Reilley on Wednesday, Jan. 26, 2022 at 2pm ET to help local reporters learn how to use basic Google tools to enhance their local news and reporting skills.

During this session, you'll be introduced to Google Fact-Check Explorer, Google Public Data Explorer, Google Trends, Google search shortcuts, MapChecking.com for crowd size estimates, Google Earth Measure tool, Google Earth Engine Timelapse.

This is a hands-on workshop and participants will receive a handout with examples, as well as links to tools and training videos. All the tools we'll be using are free.

WHEN:
Wednesday, January 26, 2022 from 2-3:00 pm ET
 
WHERE:
Zoom Meeting (link will be on the Online Event Page after you register)
 
WHO:
Mike Reilley, SPJ trainer, Google News Initiative training program
 
MORE ABOUT OUR INSTRUCTOR:
Mike is an SPJ digital trainer who has taught Google News Initiative tools to more than 9,500 journalists and educators in 280 trainings in 40 states over the past six years. He also is co-founder of and trainer with Penny Press Digital LLC, a consulting and training company. When he’s not doing trainings, he teaches data and multimedia journalism at the University of Illinois-Chicago, where he is a full-time faculty member. 
 
A former reporter at the LA Times and web editor at the Chicago Tribune, Mike served for 13 years as a faculty member at Northwestern, Arizona State University and DePaul University, teaching digital journalism to hundreds of students and professional journalists. He holds journalism degrees from the University of Nebraska-Lincoln (undergrad) and Northwestern University (masters). Mike founded and updates the research site The Journalist’s Toolbox (journaliststoolbox.org) for SPJ and runs the Chicago data site, The Red Line Project (redlineproject.org). 
 
Reach him on Twitter: https://twitter.com/journtoolbox
Reach him via email: mikereilley1@gmail.com

Event title: Info sessions about the NJ Civic Information Consortium grant process
About:
Board members will answer questions about the grant process for the NJ Civic Information Consortium.The NJ Civic Information Consortium is a first in the nation initiative to grow access to local news and information, is now accepting applications for its inaugural round of grant funding. The Consortium was created by the State of New Jersey in 2018 to grow access to civic information statewide and spark innovative media projects in communities lacking access to news and information.
During these sessions, staff members from the Center for Cooperative Media and representatives from the Consortium's board will go over the grant application process and guidelines, and will answer questions from NJ residents and organizations that are interested in applying for funding.
The Consortium will consider applications from all parts of the state, but is especially interested in proposals that focus on better serving communities of color, immigrant communities and rural communities. 
The Consortium is focusing on the following key areas for these grants, based on current pressing information needs of New Jersey residents: 
Growing and strengthening the local news ecosystem, with a particular focus on new or existing news and information outlets and projects led by and serving communities of color, immigrant communities, non-English speaking communities, and other systematically marginalized communities. With these grants, the Consortium aims to fill geographic gaps in community news and information across the state as well as gaps for communities of people not well-served by existing news outlets.Diversifying New Jersey’s journalism pipeline, media education and journalism/storytelling training in communities that lack trusted news sources by supporting outlets and projects. These grants will focus on expanding who gets to tell stories, making sure that media is more representative of the race, gender, age, and socioeconomic status of people who live in New Jersey. Additionally, in this round of grants, preference will be given to support middle and high school journalism programs.Nonpartisan civic voter information, election reporting, and election-related projects, particularly those that address mis- and disinformation and seek to get trustworthy, relevant, and timely information to the public toward more informed and participatory elections. NJCIC is seeking proposals for nonpartisan projects and reporting that foster a more informed and engaged public around local and state elections.The Consortium is seeking to support reporting and civic engagement projects focused on two specific pressing topics: the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic, and the climate crisis. Recognizing these as two urgent issues for the State of New Jersey, the Consortium aims to support solutions-focused COVID-19 and climate crisis reporting and civic engagement projects.Still have questions? Contact the Consortium via email at info@njcivicinfo.org.

Event title: Press briefing: Gun safety and violence prevention in New Jersey
About:
Join this press briefing on gun violence prevention with Rev. Robert Moore of the Coalition for Peace Action.The recent mass shootings in Uvalde, Texas and Buffalo, New York have put the spotlight, again, on the U.S. as the country that houses more firearms than people. There are more than 393 million civilian-owned guns in the U.S. — which makes up about 46 percent of the entire stock of 857 million civilian firearms in the world.
In 2020 alone, America showed an unprecedented surge in firearm purchases, including a more than 300 percent increase in permit applications in New Jersey. Gun murders have increased in the state and across the country, and hate-crimes against Asian Americans also surged to more than 3,800 last year. 
What can we do in New Jersey? Nationally? What laws should be passed to keep these weapons of destruction out of civilian hands? How should policy makers tackle gun violence? 
Please join us in a press event with Rev. Robert Moore, executive director of Coalition for Peace Action, who oversees the organization's Ceasefire NJ gun violence prevention project.

About:
Join this informational session at the Nuanced Cafe in Camden, NJ on 7/14 to learn about the restorative narrative reporting project, Stories Invincible.
Camden’s residents are rarely portrayed in the media in the same way they see themselves and their communities. Much in the way that true journalism must always come from the heart of a story, the story of any city is always within the heart of its people. Those close enough to Camden know that what truly defines the city is an unshakable, unmistakable, “invincible” spirit — and that is the story that needs to be told.
The program is supported by a subgrant from the Community Info Coop in partnership with the Movement Alliance Project, thanks to original funding from the Geraldine R. Dodge Foundation and the New Jersey Local News Lab Fund, a partnership between Dodge, Democracy Fund and the Community Foundation of New Jersey.

Event title: Dealing with fact-deniers: Engagement across the political spectrum
About:
Dealing with fact-deniers: How journalists can engage meaningfully with people across the political spectrum 
For journalists to serve democracy effectively, we need to understand and engage with people across a variety of world views.
It's part of the job of a journalist these days to demonstrate what makes our work credible. But what does that mean when we're dealing with people who don't agree on the basic facts we're reporting? How should we respond (and when should we not bother responding)? 
For journalists to serve democracy effectively, we need to understand and engage with people across a variety of world views. 
Join Joy Mayer of Trusting News to talk about what that looks like specifically as we cover the 2022 election cycle — under the persistent shadow of misinformation around 2020 results.

Event title: Panel: Covering democracy and the New Jersey midterm elections
About:
Don't miss this virtual panel on Tuesday, Oct. 18 at 2 p.m. about democracy and mid-term elections in the state of New Jersey.Let's talk about the midterms in New Jersey.
Join host Tom Moran, editorial page editor and columnist for the Star-Ledger, and special invited guests including Dr. Linda J. Caldwell Epps, a public servant, historian, and President, and CEO of 1804 Consultants, which focuses on the advancement of educational and cultural organizations. More panelists to be announced.
Among the NJ midterm elections are races to fill the state's 12 seats in the U.S. House of Representatives. While NJ Democrats control 10 of those seats, history has shown that members of the president's party tend to lose their congressional seats. Recent population change and redistricting may also sway NJ congressional races in favor of Republican candidates. 
In the run-up to the November elections, this event will provide an opportunity to discuss these pivotal races, as well as local school board, city council, and other municipal elections across the state. Our panelists will weigh in on recent political developments, and issues on the ballot and of concern to New Jerseyans, including historic inflation rates, redistricting, voter disenfranchisement, super PAC donations, campaign ad messaging, and more. PLUS, we'll hear from political science, public policy, and political history experts who are not often cited and can offer fresh perspectives that reflect New Jersey's diverse communities. 
The "Democracy and the NJ midterm elections" event is part of the "Not Your Same Old Sources" (NYSOS) series, which features experts from our Diverse Sources Database, a publicly available, sortable list of experts of different races and genders, ages, and ethnicities. 
This panel is presented by the Center for Cooperative Media based at Montclair State University in partnership with NJ.com, NJ ethnic and community media, and event sponsor, Princeton University's Office of Communications.

Climate change headlines dominated U.S. media in the aftermath of Hurricane Fiona in Puerto Rico and Hurricane Ian, which severely flooded coastal towns in New Jersey.  A year since Hurricane Ida reaped destruction on New Jersey’s densely populated cities, caused millions in damaged infrastructure and claimed thirty lives, recovery continues in the state, which has received $873.6 million in federal disaster relief funds.

Don’t miss this important discussion about climate change state and local government disaster readiness, infrastructure plans, and other issues — such as the new $52 billion contract for New York-New Jersey waterway barriers proposed by the Army Corps, and flooding vulnerabilities in low-income areas of our state. You’ll also come away with insight from community organizers, environmental justice advocates, and policy experts who are not often cited and can offer fresh perspectives that reflect New Jersey's diverse communities.
This event is part of the "Not Your Same Old Sources"  (NYSOS) series, featuring experts from our Diverse Sources Database, a publicly available, sortable list of experts of different races and genders, ages, and ethnicities.

Event title: Press event: Diversifying New Jersey's legislature copy
About:
Let's talk about diversity, representation, and New Jersey's legislature. 
Recent elections have helped, but as one of the most diverse states in the nation, NJ has a long way to go in creating a legislative body truly representative of its population. 
In 2022, the legislature welcomed 17 new members – among them two women who became the first Muslims elected to the New Jersey Legislature and the first Korean-American woman in the legislative body. Yet, women, roughly half of the state's population, represented just 30% of state legislators. And Asian (2%) and Latino (8%)representation remains low in NJ's senate and assembly. Asians make up 10 percent of NJ's population, while Latinos make up 22 percent.
Despite small gains, the state government remains dominated by white men representing 84 of 120 state senators and Assembly members.
This NYSOS event looks at how journalists cover elections and how the press can go beyond horse race coverage to examine issues of representation and access to the political processes in our state. Critics point to challenges such as gatekeeping among the state's political bosses, down-ballot voting, and low voting engagement, especially among immigrant communities. However, efforts to recruit and build up a diverse pool of candidates, including the political education and preparation of youth and others who may aspire to run for office. How are news outlets reporting on the hurdles and potential inroads to creating a more diverse legislature?   
For this panel, we're joined by host Sophie Nieto-Muñoz of The New Jersey Monitor, who will speak with our guest panel about recent demographic gains, and how communities are responding to underrepresentation in the state legislature. 
Plus, we'll hear from Assemblywoman Ellen J. Park (D), District 37 about her experience in successfully running for a seat in NJ's legislature.
The "Diversifying New Jersey's legislature" event is part of the "Not Your Same Old Sources" (NYSOS) series, featuring experts from our Diverse Sources Database, a publicly available, sortable list of experts of different races and genders, ages, and ethnicities.

Event title: Panel discussion: New report on the state of New Jersey's ethnic media
About:
Join us for a virtual panel discussion on the state of New Jersey's ethnic and community media.

Don't miss this opportunity to hear from leading voices in the field and to learn more about the state of New Jersey's ethnic and community press. This discussion will be centered around a new report, authored by Oni Advincula of the Center for Cooperative Media. 

The report offers a comprehensive look at the current state of ethnic and community media in New Jersey, highlighting both the challenges and opportunities facing these important voices in our state's media landscape.

Maintaining a healthy and strong local news ecosystem is crucial for the well-being of any community. This is particularly true when it comes to ethnic and community media outlets, which play a vital role in providing representation and giving voice to diverse perspectives and experiences. These outlets are often the first and sometimes only source of news and information for many communities, and they serve as a valuable platform for fostering dialogue and promoting understanding.

In addition to their social and cultural value, ethnic and community media outlets are also essential for the functioning of our democracy. They provide independent, reliable journalism that is vital for holding public officials and institutions accountable, and for ensuring that all members of a community have access to the information they need to make informed decisions. By supporting these outlets and promoting a vibrant local news ecosystem, we can help ensure that all members of our community have a voice and that our democracy remains strong and healthy.

Our panelists will include:
- Kenneth Miles of the Trenton Journal
- Rong Xiaoqing of Sing Tao Daily
- Kleibeel Marcano of Reporte Hispano

These panelists bring a wealth of experience and knowledge to the discussion, and will offer valuable insights and perspectives on the findings of the report.

The virtual panel will take place on January 25, 2023 at 1:00 pm ET. This event is free and open to the public, but registration is required in order to attend.

Event title: Documentary screening: Black in the Newsroom by Media 2070
Location: 1 Normal Ave. Presentation Hall, SCM Building, Montclair, NJ 07043
About:
Join us on 2/16 for a screening of Black in the Newsroom and a discussion with journalists of color from New Jersey's local news ecosystem.
Join us at Montclair State University's Presentation Hall for a screening of  Black in the Newsroom, the Media 2070 project's new documentary. 
The documentary follows the story of Elizabeth Montgomery, "a talented journalist who earns a dream job in a major Arizona newsroom — only to face deeply entrenched anti-Blackness that threatens her future." The film seeks to start a conversation about how the media industry treats Black journalists and the implications for the broader community.
After the screening, we'll hear from the filmmakers and members of the Media 2070 team, as well as local NJ journalists. 
We'll talk about issues including pay equity for journalists of color, harms perpetuated by the U.S. media system, and how we envision this system can atone for those injustices and move us to a more just and representative one. 
To learn more about this film, visit BlackInTheNewsroom.com or Media 2070. 
You can also watch a trailer of the film here.

Event title: Press event: Drug decriminalization and NJ's growing cannabis industry
About:
Let's talk about drug decriminalization and the legalized marijuana industry in New Jersey.
Recreational marijuana was legalized in New Jersey in 2020 via a ballot referendum. It took two years for the market to officially open in April 2022. Since then the industry has flourished, with sales from July to September 2022 totaling over $177 million, and generating $7.7 million in cannabis tax revenue for the state.
Legalization is certainly profitable - especially for the cluster of national cannabis companies that dominate the new market. But entrepreneurs of color and smaller shops face significant hurdles in establishing their businesses. And advocates say a key goal of legalization has gone unaddressed: healing the harm caused by the War on Drugs, which disproportionately affected Black and Latino/Hispanic people.
As the market continues to expand, different organizations and agencies are playing a role in shaping its future. Groups including the ACLU-NJ, the New Jersey Economic Development Authority, and the New Jersey Cannabis Regulatory Commission, are pushing for regulations that increase competition and promote social equity to create more opportunities for small businesses, especially those led by people of color. 
Conversations about legalization and equity in the cannabis market are important during its early stages of development. And by closely monitoring the industry and participating in discussions about regulation, New Jerseyans can help ensure that the promise of supporting local businesses is upheld.
How the media covers the cannabis industry plays a key role in informing the public about these issues and tracking state accountability.
Join our host Jelani Gibson of NJ Cannabis Insider, a section of NJ.com & The Star-Ledger, and our guest panel as they discuss the impact of weed legalization, and drug decriminalization in New Jersey.
The "Diversifying New Jersey's legislature" event is part of the "Not Your Same Old Sources" (NYSOS) series, featuring experts from our Diverse Sources Database, a publicly available, sortable list of experts of different races and genders, ages, and ethnicities.
This panel is presented by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University, in partnership with nj.com, NJ ethnic and community media, and event sponsor, Princeton University's Office of Communications.

Event title: Press event: Asylum-seekers, refugees and undocumented immigrants in NJ
About:
A press briefing for NJ News Commons members on where New Jersey stands on immigrationLet's talk about immigration in New Jersey.
Since Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis and Texas Gov. Greg Abbott started sending undocumented immigrants and asylum-seekers to “sanctuary cities,” at least 50,000 new migrants have made their way to the New York and New Jersey areas. 
Most of them have been in the state or city’s care, while waiting for their papers to be processed. By some estimates this could cost the city or state about $1 billion this fiscal year. But beyond the complicated work documentation process that puts asylum-seekers, refugees and undocumented immigrants in limbo, a chain of issues has also emerged: housing, medical care, food and other support to make ends meet are challenging for them and the state. 
Last year, NJ Gov. Murphy announced that “we are prepared” to accept migrants and “won’t use them as political pawns.” Since then, the state has offered primary program services for asylum-seekers, refugees and economic migrants, including cash and medical assistance, access to English-language classes, vocational and employment assistance. However, advocates say the state is not doing enough to promote these programs. 
This press briefing will give journalists deep insights into the number of migrants who have settled in New Jersey since 2022. We’ll provide fresh angles on new data and tips for covering immigration and immigrants, the challenges—and opportunities—the state is facing, and the impacts of separation and reunification on families and communities. 
This event is part of the "Not Your Same Old Sources" (NYSOS) series, featuring experts from our Diverse Sources Database, a publicly available, sortable list of experts of different races and genders, ages, and ethnicities.
This panel is presented by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University, in partnership with NJ.com, NJ ethnic and community media, and event sponsor, Princeton University's Office of Communications.

Event title: Press event: Hate crimes and community safety in New Jersey
About:
A press briefing for NJ News Commons members on hate crimes and community safety in New Jersey. C﻿lick here to join the Zoom.

Let's talk about hate crimes and community safety in New Jersey.

Hate crimes are on the rise across the country, and NJ is no exception.

The latest statistics from the Federal Bureau of Investigations show more than 200 bias incidents occurred in New Jersey during the year 2021. These attacks were motivated by discrimination based on race and ethnicity, religion, and sexual orientation. However only a fraction of law enforcement agencies --177 of 578 -- submitted data in 2021.

The state numbers are higher and far worse, according to the Office of the Attorney General, which said 2021 set a record high for reported bias incidents. That year, an estimated 1,871 hate crimes took place in NJ.

Based on preliminary data from the AG's office:
- Anti-Black and anti-Jewish bias were the most common motivations for reported bias incidents in 2021.
- Anti-Black bias accounted for 39% of all reported motivations in both 2020 and 2021.
- Reported incidents of Anti-Asian bias increased by 87% from 2020 to 2021.
- Anti-Jewish bias accounted for 17% of all reported motivations in 2020 and 15% in 2021.
- There were 373 reported bias incidents motivated by bias against LGBTQ+ people in 2021, up 64% from 2020.
- Reported incidents involving anti-transgender bias increased by 171% from 2020 to 2021.
Join host NJ Spotlight News Social Justice reporter, Taylor Jung and invited guests for a timely and crucial discussion on hate crimes and community safety in New Jersey. We'll hear from community leaders about their work in helping to ensure that New Jerseyans feel safe in their communities and have access to vital needs, services, and recourse within the justice system. 

We'll also discuss the response and solutions they'd like to see from state officials and law enforcement in response to the spike in hate crimes in NJ.

Event title: Press event: New Jersey communities and police accountability
About:
Join us for a press briefing about where New Jersey stands on police reform and accountability.
In the city of Paterson, after the deadly police shooting of Najee Seabrooks in March, community members grieve, protest, and demand police accountability. In response, New Jersey's attorney general, Matthew J. Platkin has since taken direct control of the city’s police department.
Seabrooks, 31, was a dedicated activist for violence intervention and had called the police during a state of mental crisis. His death brings greater scrutiny to law enforcement in New Jersey's third-largest city, and a documented history of violence and misconduct by the Paterson Police Department - actions that have disproportionately impacted Black men.
Seabrooks’ death and the fallout in Paterson is one tragic example of many and has underscored the urgency of police reform in a state where police departments have agreed to pay nearly $90 million to settle misconduct claims. A recent NJ Advance Media investigation also found that 16 of those payouts were worth at least $1 million.
New figures from the New Jersey Attorney General’s Office third-ever Major Discipline Report show that at least 404 NJ police officers faced major disciplinary action and were suspended for more than five days, demoted or terminated in 2022. This information is available to the public in an effort to increase transparency about police discipline and use of force. But community members argue that transparency is just one step towards fostering safer communities and holding officers responsible for their actions.
On Thursday June 22, join host Jelani Gibson of NJ.com, in conversation with panelists including Dr. Michael Mitchell, Assistant Professor of African American Studies and Criminology, The College of New Jersey and Alejandro Gimenez Santana, Assistant Professor of Professional Practice, Rutgers University for an impactful discussion about issues of police reform, community healing, agency and recourse when confronting abuses of law enforcement.
This press briefing will cover the following areas:
- Understanding the current state of police reform: Attendees will gain valuable insight into the current status of police reform initiatives in New Jersey, particularly in the wake of Najee Seabrooks' shooting, along with an analysis of the actions taken by Attorney General Matthew J. Platkin to increase control over the Paterson Police Department.
- Exploring the impact of police misconduct: With an exploration of the disciplinary actions against NJ police officers in 2022, and the significant payouts made to settle misconduct claims, attendees will get a comprehensive view of the consequences and societal impact of police misconduct.
- The necessity of transparency and accountability: Attendees will learn about the ongoing debates on the efficacy of increased transparency in police departments, its role in fostering safer communities, and how it aids in holding officers accountable for their actions.
- The role of community in healing and agency: Attendees can expect an enriching discussion on the role of community healing in the aftermath of police violence, with a focus on agency and recourse for communities that are confronting abuses of law enforcement.

Event title: Press event: COVID-19 and impact on NJ schools
About:
A press briefing about COVID-19 and learning loss in New Jersey schools
Three years since the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, New Jersey schools are still grappling with learning loss and disrupted classroom environments.
Students lost a reported seven years of progress during the pandemic, based on NJ Student Learning Assessments conducted by the Department of Education. Prior to the pandemic, student proficiency levels were on the incline from 2015 - 2019. After some delay, the NJDOE eventually published the 2021-2022 school performance reports this past April, making the test result available to the public.
Key findings include:
- Proficiency rates declined across all student demographics, in almost every test — specifically English Language Arts, Mathematics and Science
- 51.1% of students did not meet grade expectations in English Language Arts
- 64.6% did not meet grade expectations in mathematics
The DOE recognized the report’s limitations: “Our annual School Performance Reports provide a wealth of information that can be used by the community to engage in thoughtful conversations,” said Dr. Angelica Allen-McMillan, Acting Commissioner of Education in a press release. “While this data does not fully capture the unique circumstances and efforts of each school community, these reports are one important tool that can be used to identify successes and challenges, engage in dialogue, and work collectively toward improving the education and supports provided to all students.”
The state has sought to mitigate the impact of the pandemic on student learning by seeking to close the digital divide, ensuring that all public school students had a laptop or tablet and could access the internet. And while 358,212 did receive access to such educational resources, researchers say the divide still persists in New Jersey and nationwide.
And initiatives such as the New Jersey Partnership for Success, which seeks to enlist 5,000 volunteers to mentor and tutor students, and the High Impact Tutoring program, which will focus on tutoring services for third and fourth graders, aim to improve learning outcomes. But even as these programs roll out, there are mental health, accessibility, and other socioeconomic factors that have stressed teachers, students, and their family members as well.
On Wednesday July 19, join host Tina Kelly, Senior education reporter for NJ.com, in conversation with panelists including Dr. MinSoo Kim-Bossard, Associate Professor of Early Childhood Education at The College of New Jersey for a critical discussion about COVID-19 and learning loss issues such as testing, teacher and student mental health, absenteeism and student discipline, and barriers to learning for students with disabilities.
This event is part of the "Not Your Same Old Sources" (NYSOS) series, featuring experts from our Diverse Sources Database, a publicly available, sortable list of experts of different races and genders, ages, and ethnicities.
This panel is presented by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University, in partnership with NJ.com, NJ ethnic and community media, and event sponsor, Princeton University's Office of Communications.

Event title: Framing the media narrative on environmental justice and the climate crisis
About:
How to meet community information needs and amplify calls for accountability.

Our media shapes the public discourse about climate issues and provides life-saving information during climate-related emergencies. To that end, organizers and policymakers play an important role in providing journalists with accurate information on how to respond to it. In this interactive two-part panel discussion, participants will hear from environmental justice organizers and journalists who are knowledgeable about water and air quality issues, flooding, natural disasters, and how environmental racism impacts the health of New Jerseyans across the state.

This is especially timely given the mandate from the newly published report from the UN Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, which calls for more aggressive action to combat the crisis. Panelists at this event will share information about the policy solutions being considered to combat the crisis and discuss how journalists can be a force for good amid these challenges.

Learnings/takeaways that attendees can expect to leave the event with:
- Understanding environmental justice and the media's role: Attendees will grapple with the principles of environmental justice, including environmental racism's impact on communities. They will also understand the vital the role media plays in shaping public discourse around climate issues and environmental justice.
- Interpreting climate policies and UN's call to action: Participants will gain insights into current policy solutions aimed at addressing the climate crisis, interpreting implications of the recent UN Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change report.
- Tools and strategies for effective climate and environmental journalism: Attendees will learn strategies for effectively and equitably reporting on environmental and climate justice issues. They'll learn how to frame certain narratives that amplify calls for accountability, meet community information needs, highlight solutions, and potentially influence policy and public opinion.

This conversation will explain how newsrooms can build relationships with youth organizers as both vital sources and reporters ahead of the 2024 election cycle, unpacking how to cover youth politics and movements without patronizing and isolating those groups.
Our speakers include Teen Vogue editors Allegra Kirkland and Lexi McMenamin, cultural strategist Dillon Bernard, and college senior Mira Sydow, who uses a background in labor, climate, and educator organizing to inform her reporting.
Together, they will share tips on how to build relationships with local youth leaders, reframe your reporting to consider young people as political equals, and empower youth organizers to use journalism to tell their own stories.

- Formulating ways to build genuine relationships with local youth organizers: Attendees will learn how to cultivate meaningful source relationships with you organizers at the local level, emphasizing the role of non-transactional, community-driven newsgathering.
- Exploring the role of youth organizers as citizen journalism: Attendees will leave with a framework for how to empower youth organizers to view journalism as a truth-telling method, and how to provide feedback to novice journalists.

Event title: Let's talk about racial representation in the NJ legislature and elections
About:
Join us on 10/13 for a discussion about diversity and representation the 2023 NJ general elections.

New Jersey is one of the most diverse states in the country, yet that diversity isn't reflected in Trenton. With white men predominantly making decisions, most communities of color are underrepresented. As journalists, how can we bridge this gap and be the catalyst for change?

🗳️ Elections matter:
We'll provide an overview of the NJ general elections 2023, including how to access “election explainers” in various languages. We'll also spotlight races with diverse candidates running for the state senate and assembly.

🎯 Critical community issues:
We'll delve into the most pressing issues affecting communities of color that influence their voting decisions. This is a chance to align your reporting with the real concerns of your audience.

Here's what you can expect to take away from this briefing:
1️⃣ An overview of the state of representation in NJ:
- New Jersey’s population is 52.9% white alone, not Hispanic or Latino, and 49% male. However, of the 120 state senators and members of the Assembly who will make up the 219th Legislature, 70% — or 84 of them — are white, non-Hispanic, and two-thirds are men. This means that white people are overrepresented by about 17 percentage points and white men are overrepresented by about 34 percentage points in the state legislature compared to the state population.
- Women make up roughly half of NJ's population but only 30% of state legislators.
- Latino (8%) and Asian (2%) representation in NJ's Legislature is abysmally low, not reflecting their 22% and 10% share in the state’s population.
- Black lawmakers comprise about 15% of the Legislature, which is relatively in line with the 12% Black population in the state.
T﻿he ideal vs reality: If the New Jersey Legislature were a mirror of its population, it would reflect a far more colorful and rich tapestry.

2️⃣ Election 101 primer:
Tools and resources for “election explainers” in multiple languages to engage a broader audience.

3️⃣ Races to watch:
Key races featuring diverse candidates and their potential impact on state senate and assembly compositions.

4️⃣ Issues that matter:
Discover the most pressing concerns affecting communities of color, from healthcare to education, and how these influence voting decisions.

🗞️ The enduring impact of journalism:
While it's too late to change who's on the ballot, the role of journalism remains pivotal. Media has the power to spotlight emerging leaders, elucidate laws affecting voter rights, and demystify the democratic process for diverse communities. The stories you tell and the voices you amplify can shape public opinion and influence the trajectory of local politics.

Jacquelyn A. Suárez is the acting commissioner the NJ Department of Community Affairs. Since she began leading the DLGS in January 2021, Jacquelyn A. Suárez has served as an advocate for local government interests and provided them with technical and financial assistance in a number of areas, including joint services, purchasing, and management issues. She has also overseen the review and approval of all municipal, county, and fire district budgets, worked to uphold the financial integrity of local government units, and guided the conduct of local government officials. Event title: Analyzing New Jersey state agency ad spending
About:
Learn how Rebuild Local News is working with New Jersey news orgs to advocate that more state ad dollars go to local news organizations.
Local news has unique benefits for local economies and democracy. However, many local news organizations are in financial crisis. How can government policy help sustain the most trusted and essential information sources in a community?
One policy solution is to prioritize and direct state agency advertising money toward local news outlets instead of to tech platforms and out-of-state corporations. More cities and states are introducing such policies.
Rebuild Local News invites New Jersey news publishers and stakeholders to an information session explaining the government advertising policy approach and how New Jersey local news companies could benefit from it. 
An early analysis shows that very little of state agencies' $32M ad spend last year went to local, independent or ethnic media organizations. We'll talk extensively about how we can advocate to change that, and how individual news organizations and journalists can be involved.
The online session will be on Nov. 28 at 1 p.m. EST.
You can find RLN's policy kit and model bills at https://www.rebuildlocalnews.org/wip-government-advertising-local-news

Event title: Press briefing with acting New Jersey State Comptroller Kevin Walsh
About:
The Office of the State Comptroller (OSC) is an independent government watchdog agency that uncovers fraud, waste, abuse, and corruption. As the Acting State Comptroller, Kevin Walsh leads OSC and its investigatory oversight of more than 2,000 public entities in New Jersey, including state agencies, local governments, law enforcement agencies, and school boards.
A former civil rights attorney who led the Fair Share Housing Center, Walsh is focused on promoting efficiency, transparency, and accountability in government.
Recent reports and investigations have addressed:
- Financial mismanagement at New Jersey City University. OSC's recommendations and findings led to recent legislation strengthening state oversight of New Jersey’s state colleges and universities.
- Misspent COVID funding. Nearly half of the roughly $14 million in COVID-19 relief funds paid to New Jersey marine fisheries might be improper. As a result of the OSC's report, New Jersey's Department of Environmental Protection agreed to review the payments and recoup those found to be improper.
- Widespread disregard by local NJ police departments of key police misconduct-reporting requirements.
- The 12 lowest-rated nursing homes in New Jersey, which received more than $100 million a year in Medicaid funds.
- Unlawful payments to public employees for sick leave. OSC found that 95% of 60 towns surveyed were violating laws on sick leave payouts.
In this webinar on January 11, Walsh will discuss recent reports and offer tips for reporters on investigating fraud, waste, and abuse in local government.

More about our speaker:
Prior to joining OSC in 2020, Kevin Walsh spent two decades as a civil rights attorney and as the Executive Director of Fair Sharing Housing Center, an organization that advocates for affordable housing and represents lower-income New Jersey households impacted by disasters, among other issues.
T﻿he latest from the OSC:
The New Jersey OSC recently published a letter accusing nine NJ towns of not amending sick and vacation leave policies and hinted at advising the governor to withhold state aid from these municipalities. This follows a 14-month review showing 57 out of 60 towns not fully compliant with laws from 2007 and 2010 regarding leave payouts. Despite most towns working on corrective actions, nine towns didn't respond to the requests for reforms, leaving their plans deficient in various areas like independent evaluations of improper payouts and governing body approval of the corrective action plans.

Event title: How to raise revenue for your 2024 democracy coverage
About:
How newsrooms can use their service and accountability journalism in viable, democracy-minded revenue campaigns.

The 2024 presidential election will present fundamental and unprecedented challenges for our democracy, which means it will be an ideal year to reinforce journalism's role in preserving it. During this webinar, we'll explore how Spotlight PA centers the health and future of our democracy in its fundraising and revenue strategy, how that dovetails with our editorial strategy, and how we’ve made Democracy Day
a focal point. 

We'll also discuss the challenges and limitations of democracy-focused messaging. Attendees will come away with concrete fundraising and messaging examples to best capitalize on this pivotal year for our country.

- Examples of concrete fundraising and messaging strategies that effectively leverage the significance of the 2024 election year.
- Knowledge of the challenges and limitations of using democracy-focused messaging in journalism and fundraising.
- Tips from Christopher Baxter and Joanna Zuckerman Bernstein on planning fundraising campaigns, civic events, and partnerships that support public-service journalism.

Questions?

This event is part of U.S. Democracy Day 2024, a national collaborative pro-democracy reporting project aimed at highlighting threats to American democracy and reporting on solutions. Learn more and sign up to participate at usdemocracyday.org.

More about our speakers:
Spotlight PA is a nonprofit, nonpartisan newsroom producing investigative and public-service reporting about the Pennsylvania state government and urgent statewide issues with a focus on journalism that gets results.

Christopher Baxter (he/him) is the CEO/president and founding editor of Spotlight PA and oversees all aspects of the organization. His work as an investigative reporter and editor has regularly prompted meaningful change in the form of new laws, criminal investigations, resignations, and legislative hearings. Joanna Zuckerman Bernstein (she/her) is the deputy director of Spotlight PA, where she leads the fundraising team. Event title: Press event: Black voters and the 2024 elections
Featured image: Black voters and 2024 election w logos.png (https://v5.airtableusercontent.com/v3/u/51/51/1774058400000/M6Wz-IL6nXCdVaXlf4Gd_g/_u4J23Tpwq6JBxBqEjI4lsRExfhLTC5m-gDZnQlwwm8aiu2cL0ultE21BOoYHYBpXtzn2qA5lQfTUObmvD9EzcTHcspr2L10OrXD84KV9ZeBKn7UibjVTHPD2edgdCxQR5NgsEcg2US2uD5AFciQZNqxbhzjT-nQfAJ-bRed1_s/xHBlikmZwHVNxxySJxA-Im_I4mbDMO_xpbpx11FYUPY)
About:
Join us on Monday, Feb. 19 at 2 p.m. for a panel about Black voters and the 2024 elections.
REGISTER HERE: https://www.eventbrite.com/e/press-event-black-voters-and-the-2024-elections-tickets-825832215387
This panel will look at issues important to Black voters and address themes such as inflation, housing inequality, education, and voting rights. These high-priority issues are frequently covered in the news and included in campaign messaging at the national level — and for good reason.
- New Jersey is the state most impacted by foreclosures in the country: here 1 in every 794 housing units has a foreclosure filing.
- A recent study from the New Jersey Institute for Social Justice found the median household wealth for white families in New Jersey is $322,500, while the median for Black families is $17,700, and for Latino families, $26,100. The study says the state’s racial wealth gap is nearly double what it is nationally.
- New Jersey’s public school system is the sixth most segregated state for Black students and seventh for Latino students, according to researchers.
- With the John Lewis Voting Rights Act stalled in the Senate, NJ lawmakers have introduced legislation, including the New Jersey Voting Rights Act and the same-day voter registration bill, to make the voting process accessible to more New Jerseyans. Studies have shown that same-day voter registration increases turnout among Black, Latino, and young voters.

But we’ll also touch on more difficult topics that are also impacting turnout among Black voters, such as racism and white supremacy, community safety, and police reform.
For example, hate crimes are on the rise in New Jersey, and about 38% of bias incidents were spurred by anti-Black sentiments — even though Black people and African Americans only make up about 15 percent of the state’s population.
Hear from a dynamic panel, including Newark Mayor Ras Baraka, Saladin Ambar, Professor of Political Science at Rutgers University, and other special guests.
The "Black Voters and the 2024 Elections" event is part of the "Not Your Same Old Sources" (NYSOS) series, featuring experts from our Diverse Sources Database, a publicly available, sortable list of experts of different races and genders, ages, and ethnicities.
———
This panel is presented by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University, in partnership with Mosaic.nj.com, NJ.com, NJ ethnic and community media, and our event sponsor, Princeton University's Office of Communications.
———
Meet our moderator
- Dr. James Jones - Assistant Professor at Rutgers University-Newark and Director of the University’s new Center for Politics and Race in America, which seeks to fill a critical gap in our understanding of America’s political landscape by serving as a non-partisan, multidisciplinary, fact tank on the link between race and politics in the US.
Meet our panelists
- Dr. Saladin Ambar - Professor of Political Science and Senior Scholar at the Center on the American Governor at Rutgers University’s Eagleton Institute of Politics.
- Mayor Ras Baraka - Mayor of the state’s largest city and president of NJ Urban Mayors Association; executive board member for NJ League of Municipalities; co-chair, National League of Cities Reimagining Public Safety Task Force
- Ashanti Jones - Policy Analyst in the Criminal Justice Reform Program at the New Jersey Institute for Social Justice. She’s also a social worker, speaker, and policy analyst with a specialty in juvenile justice, restorative and transformative justice, and equity practices.
- Shavonda Sumter - Assemblywoman from the 35th Dist and chair of the New Jersey Legislative Black Caucus

About:

More about our speaker:
Mike Shapiro is the founder of TAPinto, an award-winning network of more than 95 independently owned and operated local news platforms. Mike is passionate about enabling individuals to own their own local news and digital marketing business, be their own boss, and achieve financial success, all while helping their community at the same time.

In 2008, he started an all-online local news site in his old hometown of New Providence, New Jersey. Soon, residents in the neighboring towns requested he start sites in their towns. After launching several more local news sites, he left his job to focus on local news full-time. 

He started to get more and more requests from people to start local news sites in their towns but he had to decline as he could not do any more sites himself. So he started thinking about how they could expand yet keep the sites local - producing high-quality, objective local news. About seven years ago, he came up with the idea of franchising local news - finding people who wanted to start TAPinto as their own business and providing them with the technology, training, support, and continuing education they needed to be successful.

TAPinto took off and today, they have more than 90 franchised online local news sites producing award-winning journalism while achieving record traffic and financial success, and are one of the only sustainable, scalable local journalism models in the country.

Event title: Press event: The housing crisis in New Jersey
About:
Join us on  March 20, 2024, at 1 p.m. for a panel on affordable housing and the housing crisis in the Garden State.Let's talk about affordable housing and the housing crisis in New Jersey.

Housing inequality has hit many NJ communities hard. And in recent years, an unstable job market, inflation, and increasing costs of living have made residents that much more vulnerable to eviction, foreclosure, and homelessness. People of color and those residing in low-income communities are also hardest hit by soaring rent and home sale prices, in addition to redlining and predatory lending practices that put homeownership out of reach.

The housing crisis is real and alarming. 
According to the ATTOM Special Housing Risk Report , New Jersey is the state most impacted by foreclosures in the country: here, 1 in every 794 housing units has a foreclosure filing.Currently, the fate of New Jersey's major affordable housing legislation is unknown. The bill, S-50, aims to overhaul how the state's housing obligations are determined by replacing the defunct Council on Affordable Housing (COAH) with a new system. The bill is part of an effort to create 200,000 additional housing units to meet the needs of low and moderate-income residents in NJ. A  recent Rutgers study found that nearly 50 percent of homes sold in Newark went to institutional investor buyers, which fueled "rapidly rising rents, decreased homeownership, higher barriers to affordable housing production goals, renter displacement and less stable communities."The state is making some strides in making affordable housing and home ownership more affordable in NJ. For example, in January, Governor Phil Murphy signed A5664/S4240 into law, creating a Community Wealth Preservation Program. This law aims to make foreclosure sales more fair and equitable by giving people — including those who have lost their homes, their families, renters, and others— the chance to buy and get financing for homes that have been foreclosed on. 

Don’t miss this NYSOS panel for a discussion of these issues and more!

We’ll be joined by host Schlonn Hawkins,  Chief Executive Officer and publisher of Shelterforce, who will speak with special invited guests about New Jersey’s housing crisis and impact on residents, in particular, communities of color.
———
This event is part of the "Not Your Same Old Sources" (NYSOS) series, featuring experts from our Diverse Sources Database, a publicly available, sortable list of experts of different races and genders, ages, and ethnicities.
This panel is presented by the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University, in partnership with NJ.com, and NJ ethnic and community media.
———

Meet our moderator
Schlonn Hawins - Chief Executive Officer and publisher of Shelterforce.  . Founded in 1975, Shelterforce "uses the power of journalism to inspire, inform, and hold accountable all those working toward more just, equitable, and thriving communities where every person has a place to call home." An executive with more than 15 years of experience, Schlonn has workded at the United Way of Delaware and served as director of Delaware’s Campaign for Grade-Level Reading—Get Delaware Reading, and drove collective impact to help ensure that more children in low-income families are reading on grade level and achieving long-term educational success.
Meet our panelists
Kelvin Boddy - Director of Healthy Homes and Communities at Housing and Community Development Network of New Jersey. Boddy is responsible for collaborative development projects involving crime prevention, healthy housing, and local advocacy/empowerment.Professor John N. Robinson III - Associate Professor of Sociology at Princeton University. Robinson studies the racial underpinnings of money and markets, with emphasis on housing and credit policies. His award-winning work examines how the rise of finance is reshaping place-based inequalities within and around American cities.
Questions? Contact the Center directly at info@centerforcoperativemedia.org.

Event title: New Jersey Ethnic + Community Media Expo 2024
Location: The College of New Jersey, 200 Pennington Avenue, Brower Student Center, Room 225 Trenton, NJ 08618
About:
Join us on April 4, 2024 for the first NJ Ethnic + Community Media Expo in Trenton, NJ!
The Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University is proud to present the first-ever New Jersey Community Media Expo. This expo will unite and showcase the state’s local, ethnic, and community news outlets and journalists to lawmakers, advertising agencies, academics, marketing, and communications groups.
New Jersey is home to at least 140 ethnic and community media organizations. This includes 94 daily and weekly newspapers and periodicals, 35 radio stations, and 11 television channels, reflecting New Jersey as one of the most culturally vibrant, ethnically, racially, and linguistically diverse states in the country. These media cover stories about African, African American, Asian, European, Latino, and Middle Eastern communities, and other stories that affect or interest the myriad diaspora communities in the state. They reach people that mainstream media do not.
These community media organizations will be invited to showcase their news outlets at tables at the Expo. Attendees are invited to network with the news organizations and pick up brochures, pamphlets, media kits, and more to learn about how they can partner with ethnic and community media to reach non-white and non-English speaking residents in New Jersey.
The Expo is set for April 4. Registration and networking begin at 11 a.m. Lunch will be served at noon, followed by keynote speakers and two hours of open networking time in the Expo space. The Expo will be hosted at The College of New Jersey, 2000 Pennington Road in Ewing Township.
The Expo is supported thanks in part to funding from the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation and NEO Philanthropy. If you would like to sponsor the Expo, contact Stefanie Murray, director of the Center, at murrayst@montclair.edu.
We plan to publish the recordings, documentation, and other materials once the Expo is over, so be sure to come back to this page and check them out. Meanwhile, don’t forget to follow the Center on Twitter.
______________________
HOW TO PARTICIPATE
This event is open to the public. It is free to attend for ethnic and community media who will exhibit at the Expo. A nominal fee of $15 is requested for all other attendees.
Group discounts are available for companies wishing to send large teams of 10 or more.
Vendors and other organizations wishing to have a table in the Expo will be considered on a case-by-case basis and those accepted will pay a $100 exhibition fee.
🎟️ Ethnic and community media wishing to exhibit at the Expo should register here.

If you would like to send a team of 10 or more or are a non-ethnic media vendor wishing to have a table, email Denise Shannon at shannond@montclair.edu to arrange registration.

When you register, you will have the opportunity to tell us if you have any accessibility needs or would like to request any additional accommodations and we will take care of you!

Event title: Meet the candidates: Press briefing with Patricia Campos-Medina
About:
Meet with NJ senatorial candidate Dr. Patricia Campos-Media ahead of the 2024 election.
The race to replace Sen. Bob Menendez in the upcoming election is set to be a significant contest among candidates seeking to represent the more than 9 million residents of New Jersey. While all candidates have expressed support for Pres. Joe Biden, their diverse backgrounds and contrasting views on policy and economic issues promise to make this a fascinating and closely followed race.
As part of the ongoing "Not Your Same Old Sources" series, the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University will host a three-part series of virtual press briefings featuring New Jersey senatorial candidates.
The first briefing in the series will take place on Tuesday, April 16, and will feature senatorial candidate Dr. Patricia Campos-Medina, who will share her vision for the future of New Jersey with voters across the state.
———
Campos-Medina brings to the race three decades of experience as an advocate for American workers and unions, and she will discuss her strong positions on crucial issues such as Medicare for All, student debt relief, affordable housing, women's reproductive freedom, and peace efforts. As a dedicated champion for working families, Campos-Medina possesses a deep understanding of the challenges faced by New Jerseyans and is well-positioned to address the most pressing concerns of the state's residents.
This virtual press briefing will be hosted on Zoom, providing an opportunity for journalists to engage with Campos-Medina and gain a more comprehensive understanding of her platform and objectives as she seeks to represent the people of New Jersey in the U.S. Senate. Members of the media are encouraged to attend and ask questions to better inform their coverage of this highly anticipated election.
For more information about the "Not Your Same Old Sources" series and to RSVP for the virtual media briefing with Patricia Campos-Medina, please contact the Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University via email at info@centerforcooperativemedia.org.

Event title: Media narratives on environmental justice and the climate crisis in 2024
About:
How to meet community information needs and amplify calls for accountabilityOur media shapes the public discourse about climate issues and provides life-saving information during climate-related emergencies. To that end, organizers and policymakers play an important role in providing journalists with accurate information on how to respond to it. In this interactive two-part panel discussion, participants will hear from environmental justice organizers and journalists who are knowledgeable about water and air quality issues, flooding, natural disasters, and how environmental racism impacts the health of New Jerseyans across the state.
This is especially timely given the mandate from the newly published report from the UN Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, which calls for more aggressive action to combat the crisis. Panelists at this event will share information about the policy solutions being considered to combat the crisis and discuss how journalists can be a force for good amid these challenges.
Learnings/takeaways that attendees can expect to leave the event with:
Understanding environmental justice and the media's role: Attendees will grapple with the principles of environmental justice, including environmental racism's impact on communities. They will also understand the vital the role media plays in shaping public discourse around climate issues and environmental justice.Interpreting climate policies and UN's call to action: Participants will gain insights into current policy solutions aimed at addressing the climate crisis, interpreting implications of the recent UN Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change report.Tools and strategies for effective climate and environmental journalism: Attendees will learn strategies for effectively and equitably reporting on environmental and climate justice issues. They'll learn how to frame certain narratives that amplify calls for accountability, meet community information needs, highlight solutions, and potentially influence policy and public opinion.
— ——
This panel is presented by Free Press, the Center for Cooperative Media, and the NJ League of Conservation Votersin partnership with NJ.com, NJ ethnic and community media, and Princeton University's Office of Communications.

Event title: Covering climate change before (and beyond) the 2024 election
About:

🚨 REGISTER HERE: https://www.eventbrite.com/e/covering-climate-change-before-and-beyond-the-2024-election-tickets-951046695267

Climate change doesn’t happen in a vacuum.
The decisions of policymakers — especially at the local level — impact the United States’ ability to reduce carbon emissions, limit mass-produced waste, and recover from severe weather.
Co-produced by Grist and Democracy Day, this panel will provide local reporters with answers to some pretty big questions: What are the major climate issues that could be affected by the 2024 election? Our panel of expert climate reporters and educators will help attendees:
- Identify the environmental issues most relevant to the 2024 election and find ways to report on them locally
- Avoid framing pitfalls when covering the impacts of climate change by focusing on solutions and intersectionality
- Feel confident about holding elected officials and candidates accountable for climate policy by demystifying the connections between government and the environment.

Event title: Civic science: Enhancing trust and transparency when reporting on avian flu
About:
It's been three months since an outbreak of avian influenza in U.S. dairy cattle was declared.
So far the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) has confirmed 126 cases of bird flu in dairy cattle herds in 12 states. The states include Colorado, Idaho, Iowa, Kansas, Michigan, Minnesota, New Mexico, North Carolina, Ohio, South Dakota, Texas, and Wyoming. Idaho has the most affected dairy herds, with 27.
New Jersey is not on the list of states impacted and there are no reports of H5N1 bird flu cases in New Jersey residents or dairy cows (as of July 2024). However, the New Jersey Communicable Disease Service (CDS) remains vigilant, monitoring the situation in wild birds, poultry, and dairy cows in collaboration with state and federal partners. The CDS will continue to provide updates as new information becomes available.
Experts have voiced concerns about the U.S. needing to prepare to respond in the event of a bird flu outbreak. They caution that health officials should take preventative measures now so as to not repeat mistakes made during the COVID-19 pandemic. Experts have also highlighted that testing shortages and slow regulatory responses have left the U.S. unprepared for a major bird flu outbreak, stressing the importance of transparent and effective public health communication.
Join us for an engaging panel discussion featuring New Jersey's leading experts in public health as we explore how a civic science approach can improve transparency and trust in reporting on avian flu and communicable and other diseases.

Meet our panelists:
- Ed Wengryn, New Jersey Secretary of Agriculture
- Dr. Amar Patil, New Jersey State Veterinarian

———
This event is open to the public and free to attend. For questions, please contact the Center for Cooperative Media at info@centerforcooperativemedia.org.

———

Event title: Exposing hate: How to cover right-wing groups on your beat
About:
This webinar from former New York Times contributor Kyle Spencer will prime local journalists  on how to cover far-right groups.

ZOOM LINK: https://us06web.zoom.us/j/491182107
REGISTRATION LINK: https://www.eventbrite.com/e/exposing-hate-how-to-cover-right-wing-groups-on-your-beat-tickets-981845916567

Across the United States, groups like Moms for Liberty are coordinating efforts to take over school boards and gut public libraries of diverse books. Grassroots election deniers are pressuring rural communities to alter voting procedures, dismantling civic infrastructure, and intimidating would-be voters. If the architects of Project 2025 get their way, no government agency or municipality will be left untouched.

Facilitated by award-winning journalist and author Kyle Spencer, this hour-long training will provide local journalists with the insight, tools, background, and data-mining tips they need to uncover far-right organizations and report on how they are infiltrating — and influencing — their communities.

During this webinar, Spencer will help attendees:
- Identify the far-right organizations local reporters are most likely to encounter, as well as their common aims and organizing tactics.
- Follow the money and figure out who is funding them.
- Develop a clear plan for how to cover these groups without amplifying misinformation, fearmongering, or de-centering the efforts of those working to combat them.
- Learn how to protect themselves on and offline while covering groups that have a history of doxxing and confrontation.

This session will be conducted via Zoom. The Zoom link will be provided on the Online Event Page once you RSVP.

About our facilitator
Kyle Spencer is a former New York Times contributor and the founding editor of Reporting Right, a weekly pro-democracy journalism newsletter for local reporters and editors across the U.S. Spencer runs skills-based workshops on how to do pro-democracy journalism, combat misinformation, and intentionally keep propaganda out of the news, drawing on 20-plus years of experience as an award-winning beat reporter in Pennsylvania, North Carolina, for the Times, and at MSNBC. While reporting for her critically acclaimed book Raising Them Right: The Untold Story of America’s Ultraconservative Youth Movement and Its Plot for Power, Spencer spent hours with radicalized activists who spoke candidly about their efforts to manipulate the media. Her workshops are informed by her understanding of this phenomenon.

Event title: Why you should cover how your state certifies votes in the 2024 election
About:
Led by journalists from Votebeat, this webinar will help local reporters explain how their state plans to certify election results.When the rules in Georgia for certifying election results changed to satisfy former President Donald Trump loyalists, the process for certifying votes (which each state must do before the Electoral College proceeds) started getting national attention.
In reality, every state certifies elections differently, but the process should be undergirded by procedure, not personal judgments — even as some local officials have shown a willingness to ignore the law.
Covering how your state certifies elections is vital to building trust in democracy, which is why we’re partnering with Votebeat, a nonpartisan local election reporting organization, to equip reporters to doggedly cover the process, how to enforce it, and the challenges election officials face to prevent biases from interfering.
During this will webinar, the Votebeat team will help attendees:
Determine where to find information on their state’s election certification process, and the best ways to break down the minutiae for their community.Develop a framework for covering election certification that builds trust in democratic processes instead of sowing doubt.Map the common conspiracies around election certification so reporters can easily pre-bunk and debunk falsehoods as they ariseThis session will be conducted via Zoom. The Zoom link will be provided on the Online Event Page once you RSVP.

—
📅 Date: Tuesday, Oct. 8, 2024
🕒 Time: 1-2:00 pm ET
📍 Location: Virtual
(Zoom Link will be provided after you register. We will also send the Zoom link directly to your email multiple times before the event begins.)

Meet our instructors:
Jen Fifield is a senior reporter at Votebeat based in Phoenix. She previously covered Maricopa County for The Arizona Republic and was on the team that won the 2021 Scripps Howard Award for distinguished service to the First Amendment for their investigation into the attempts to overturn Arizona’s 2020 election results. Prior to that, she covered local government for newspapers in Maryland and state policy for Stateline. Jen is a Phoenix native and graduated from Arizona State University’s Cronkite School.
Hayley Harding is a Detroit-based reporter for Votebeat who has covered the government and the people who run it at all levels. Before that, she was a data journalist on The Detroit News’ investigations team, where she covered population trends, social problems, and more. She has worked for the Idaho Statesman in Boise and for the Salisbury Daily Times in Salisbury, Maryland. A Michigander by birth, Hayley graduated from Ohio University.
Carter Walker is Votebeat’s reporter in Pennsylvania covering voting and election administration for Votebeat. He has covered the state’s convoluted election code, election misinformation, and failures in election administration. His work was awarded the 2024 Pacesetter Award from the Election Verification Network. Prior to Votebeat, he covered extremism, misinformation, and local politics for LancasterOnline, and was a finalist for the 2023 Livingston Award.

About our host:
Carrie Levine is Votebeat's managing editor. She was previously a senior reporter for the Center for Public Integrity, where she covered voting access, money in politics and influence. She has also worked for the National Law Journal and the Charlotte Observer, as well as served as the research director at CREW, a nonpartisan watchdog group. A graduate of Boston University and the Columbia University Graduate School of Journalism, she is based in Washington, D.C.

Event title: Tools and best practices for fact-checking during elections
About:
In this RTDNA/Google News Initiative training, we’ll explore ways to fight back against misinformation and disinformation during election coverage.
We’ll use tools such as Google Fact-Check Explorer to track fact-checked images and stories. We’ll use reverse image search and other Google tools to check election claims.
We’ll break down doctored video and audio with WatchFramebyFrame and Deepfake-o-meter.
We’ll also look at the innovative Rolliapp.com to track disinformation spreaders on social channels. Participants get a handout with links to tools and exercise materials you can take to your newsroom.
Prior to the training, set up a free Rolliapp.com “press pass” account as it takes a couple of days to get approved. Use the passcode: JOURNOAI
Our instructor, Mike Reilley, is a UIC senior lecturer and founder of JournalistsToolbox.ai. You can contact him on social media @itsmikereilley or @journtoolbox.

Event title: The future of local news in Hudson County, New Jersey
About:
With the impending closure of The Jersey Journal, what is the future of news in Hudson County?After 157 years in business and being an anchor in Hudson County, The Jersey Journal will close early next year. What does that mean for the local news ecosystem in and around Jersey City and throughout the rest of the county? 
During this webinar, we'll discuss the current local media landscape through the eyes of several news entrepreneurs and organizations that have opened there in the last decade, as well as journalists who have been covering beats there for years.

Who is covering local news now in Hudson County and what kind of support do they need?What will the closure of The Jersey Journal mean and who will it impact?As we look toward the future, especially the next 5-10 years, what may be needed to better meet community information needs?

Event title: Preparing for disaster: A workshop for NJ local news organizations
Location: Montclair State University, Morehead Hall, 1 Normal Ave, Montclair, NJ 07043
About:
REGISTER HERE: https://www.eventbrite.com/e/preparing-for-disaster-a-workshop-for-nj-local-news-organizations-tickets-1115617003489
 
When disaster strikes, local newsrooms play a vital role in keeping their communities informed and safe. But are New Jersey's local news organizations prepared to continue operating when they're needed most?

Co-produced by the Center for Cooperative Media, NJVOAD, and CUNY's Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism, this in-person workshop will provide local journalists and news organizations with practical disaster preparedness training and resources to ensure they can continue serving their communities during emergencies.

Workshop participants will:
- Learn how to create and implement continuity of operations plans (COOP) for their organizations
- Understand best practices for emergency communications and staff safety
- Receive hands-on training with essential disaster preparedness equipment
- Take home a disaster preparedness go-bag ($250 value) containing crucial emergency supplies
- Network with emergency management professionals and fellow journalists
NOTE: Space is limited to 30 participants. Priority will be given to journalists from smaller outlets and those serving historically underserved communities.
