Event title: Let's talk about affordable housing and the housing crisis in New Jersey
About:
The housing crisis in New Jersey has reached alarming levels, with communities of color and low-income residents bearing the brunt of soaring costs, predatory practices, and institutional barriers to homeownership. The state now ranks 6th nationally in foreclosure rates, with one foreclosure for every 3,442 housing units – hitting Cumberland, Camden, and Gloucester counties particularly hard.
Recent research from Rutgers University found that nearly 50% of homes sold in Newark went to institutional investors, fueling rapidly rising rents, decreased homeownership, and higher barriers to affordable housing. While new state legislation calls for more than 146,000 affordable housing units over the next decade, many communities continue to struggle with displacement and housing instability.
Join us for an expert discussion on how reporters can cover these critical issues effectively and responsibly, with a focus on data-driven, community-centered, and solutions-oriented approaches.

Date and time:
📅 Date: Wednesday, March 19, 2025
🕒 Time: 2-3:30 pm ET
📍Location: Zoom/virtual
(Zoom Link will be provided after you register. We will also send the Zoom link directly to your email multiple times before the event begins.)

👥 Panelists and moderator to be announced soon
According to the ATTOM Special Housing Risk Report, New Jersey ranked sixth in the nation for foreclosure rates with one foreclosure for every 3,442 housing units. The highest foreclosure rates were seen in Cumberland, Camden, and Gloucester counties.
New Jersey recently passed legislation to better enforce affordable housing regulations reaching back to the Mount Laurel Decision of 1975. According to the doctrine, “towns are required by law to follow certain rules that safeguard access to affordable housing for low-income families and communities of color.” The new law initially called for the construction or rehabilitation of more than 146,000 residences over the next decade.
A recent Rutgers study found that nearly 50 percent of homes sold in Newark went to institutional investor buyers, which fueled "rapidly rising rents, decreased homeownership, higher barriers to affordable housing production goals, renter displacement, and less stable communities."
Our expert panel will help journalists understand how to access and interpret housing data, implement best practices for community-centered coverage, and develop solutions-oriented approaches to housing journalism. We'll explore key policy developments like the Mount Laurel doctrine, examine the role of local and state government, and discuss strategies for covering rent control and eviction protection measures.
Featured topics include the current state of housing inequality, the impact of institutional investors on local markets, the implementation of affordable housing regulations, and community-based solutions and initiatives.

–––
This event is part of the "Not Your Same Old Sources" (NYSOS) series, featuring experts from our Diverse Sources Database, a publicly available, sortable list of experts of different races and genders, ages, and ethnicities.

Event title: Let's talk about special education in NJ amid cuts to the Education Dept.
About:
Join us on 4/17 for a press briefing on special education in New Jersey amid cuts to the Department of Education.

REGISTER HERE: https://www.eventbrite.com/e/lets-talk-about-special-education-in-nj-amid-cuts-to-the-education-dept-tickets-1308593812319

On March 20, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order to “return education to the states,” triggering massive changes to the U.S. Department of Education (DOE). The administration has also called for the transfer of special education oversight to the Department of Health and Human Services. The move has alarmed special education experts, school officials, and child safety advocates in New Jersey.

Historically, the Education Department has enforced the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), protecting education rights for approximately 7.5 million children nationwide, including critical funding in support of special education and low-income students. Here in New Jersey, special education programs have received approximately $1.2 billion annually.

Loss of this critical funding could mean a shift to block grant models in which NJ and other states across the country receive federal funding in lump sums with fewer restrictions or guidelines. Advocates are concerned that such allocations could mean less accountability and uneven distribution of resources for vulnerable student populations. And ultimately, cash-strapped local districts may be forced to cut essential special education services or increase taxes on local homeowners to maintain current levels of support.

Additional factors that could affect NJ’s school system:
- The Trump administration has laid off some 1,300 DOE employees, including critical staff who conducted special education research and investigated civil rights complaints. 
- The elimination of essential research functions previously handled by the Institute of Education Sciences (IES) could have long-term impacts on the equity and effectiveness of special education programs.
- DOE cuts could further harm an education system already stressed by teacher shortages and growing student mental health crises. 

This briefing aims to clarify the current situation, communicate potential impacts on special education and child safety in New Jersey, and outline the state's ongoing efforts to safeguard students' rights and secure consistent federal support.

Event title: Press briefing: Responsible immigration coverage in 2025
About:
Immigration has become one of the most significant and contentious issues in the United States. Recent policy changes during the Trump administration, including the ending of Temporary Protected Status for Venezuelan, Haitian, and Cuban immigrants, as well as increased detention, arrests, and deportations, have had profound consequences for immigrant communities in New Jersey and across the country. As immigration grows more complex, coverage demands greater sensitivity, accuracy, and a commitment to ethical reporting.
This panel discussion will provide new insights for reporters and editors on exploring how current immigration issues intersect with other important topics, such as elections, healthcare systems, the economy, the environment, and human rights.
Our expert panelists will address:
- The rights of immigrants under the current administration
- Ethical considerations when interviewing immigrants and asylum seekers, particularly those from sensitive locations such as schools, hospitals, or places of worship
- Best practices to ensure your stories, photos, or videos don't unintentionally put individuals, including children and families, at risk
______________________
📅 Date: Wednesday, April 30, 2025
🕒 Time: 2:00 PM - 3:00 PM EDT
📍 Location: Virtual (Zoom link will be provided after registration)
______________________
Meet our panelists
- Linda Dakin-Grimm is an attorney who has represented over 100 unaccompanied migrant children and separated families within the U.S. immigration system. She has successfully challenged the first Trump administration in federal courts and is the author of "Dignity & Justice: Welcoming the Stranger at Our Border."
- Amy Torres is executive director of the New Jersey Alliance for Immigrant Justice (NJAIJ), the largest immigration coalition in the state. Under her leadership, NJAIJ has secured a state-wide ban on ICE detention contracts (the first on the East Coast and fifth in the nation), implemented a direct pandemic relief program for families excluded from federal COVID-19 stimulus, and successfully campaigned for drivers' licenses for all residents regardless of immigration status.
Moderator
Colleen O'Dea is a senior reporter at New Jersey Spotlight News/PBS.

Event title: NJ Budget transparency tool demonstration and Q&A
About:
Join us on 8/19 for a demonstration and Q&A on njbudget.com, a new tool designed to bring greater transparency to the NJ State Budget.
RSVP HERE: https://www.eventbrite.com/e/nj-budget-transparency-tool-demonstration-and-qa-tickets-1542483492649
The New Jersey state budget process can be opaque and difficult to navigate for journalists and citizens alike.
This project, funded by the NJ Civic Information Consortium, focuses on the hundreds of line items added to the state budget annually—those "extras" that often go unnoticed but can significantly impact local communities. The new njbudget.com tool aims to change that by making budget data more accessible and searchable than ever before.
Our demonstration will help journalists and civic-minded residents learn how to use this powerful new resource called "Between the Lines." This tool provides transparency into hundreds of millions of taxpayer dollars within the NJ state budget awarded to local governments and nonprofit organizations without any public process or merit-based criteria. Now, anyone can plainly find out how much your county, your legislative district, your municipality, or any organization receives from this unsupervised portion of the NJ state budget.
The intuitive search functions allow you to easily find budget "extras" by county, municipality, or organization, making it an essential tool for research and reporting. The site includes comprehensive data from FY24, FY25, and the Governor's current recommendation for FY26.
We'll walk you through the site's key features, demonstrate search functions, and answer any questions you have about its functionality and potential applications for your work.
__________
📅 Date: Tuesday, August 19, 2025
🕒 Time: 1:00 PM-2:00 PM EST
📍 Location: Virtual (Zoom)
This session is ideal for journalists, editors, researchers, civic organizations, and anyone interested in government transparency and accountability.
__________
About our presenter
Audrey Lane is president of Garden State Initiative, a public relations and communications professional focused on positive change through strong policy. She is known for her advocacy and creative awareness campaigns working with grassroots organizations, legislators, and organizations at the local and state level.
Lane previously held the role of Executive Director for Jersey1st where she developed campaigns for New Jersey families and businesses focused on issues including education, overregulation, and community safety. She has extensive experience in local government, having served as a Council member in Mountain Lakes, NJ on multiple committees including Financial Advisory, Environmental Commission, and Planning and Zoning Boards.
Lane has served as Communications Director and Campaign Manager on New Jersey political campaigns and was named as a "2022 Right Women to Watch" by Save Jersey Media. She graduated from Johns Hopkins University, where she was a student athlete and recipient of the Louis Azrael Fellowship in Communications.
Follow Garden State Initiative: Facebook | Twitter | LinkedIn

Supported by Google News Initiative, this content-sharing network represents a major infrastructure investment in New Jersey's local journalism ecosystem, addressing the top concerns identified by newsrooms in our 2025 spring survey: easier content access and revenue generation support.

Event title: Media liability insurance for NJ local news orgs
About:
Join us for an informational webinar about affordable media liability insurance options specifically designed for small newsrooms.
RSVP HERE: https://www.eventbrite.com/e/media-liability-insurance-for-nj-local-news-orgs-tickets-1708596471039?aff=oddtdtcreator

ZOOM LINK: https://us06web.zoom.us/j/85076176419Small newsrooms across the country have long struggled with inadequate or unaffordable media liability insurance, leaving them vulnerable to potentially crippling legal costs from libel, defamation, and other media-related lawsuits. A new insurance program through AXIS Insurance offers a solution designed specifically for smaller news organizations.
The policy is being offered by Axis, a leading insurer with an "A" rating from AM Best and an "A+" from Standard & Poor's, which has offered coverage in this space for more than 45 years, in partnership with EPIC Insurance Brokers & Consultants. This program addresses key problems that have plagued smaller newsrooms: high deductibles that can force self-censorship, limited choice in legal counsel, and policies that allow insurers to settle cases without the newsroom's consent.
For newsrooms with annual budgets under $2 million, the retention will be an affordable $2500, significantly lower than many existing policies. The coverage includes broad-based publisher's liability protection for claims involving defamation, invasion of privacy, slander, copyright infringement, and subpoena defense.
Join us for this informational webinar where Brian Finnell from EPIC Insurance will explain the program details, application process, and answer questions about coverage options for New Jersey news organizations.
This webinar will help you:
- Understand the importance of media liability insurance for local newsrooms
- Learn about new, more affordable coverage options with lower deductibles
- Navigate the application process and requirements
- Ask questions about specific coverage needs and scenarios
This webinar follows the July 2025 announcement of improved media liability insurance options for smaller newsrooms, offering coverage through AXIS Insurance with more favorable terms than previously available.
__________
📅 Date: Tuesday, September 23, 2025
🕒 Time: 1:00-2:00 PM EDT
📍 Location: Virtual (Zoom link will be provided upon registration)
__________
Why your newsroom needs media insurance
In today's climate of increasing hostility toward journalism and declining resources for newsrooms, media liability insurance isn't just recommended—it's essential for survival. Here's why:
- Legal costs can be devastating: Even meritless lawsuits can generate tens of thousands of dollars in legal fees. A single case could force a small newsroom to drastically cut operations or close entirely.
- Defense costs often exceed damages: In media cases, the most expensive costs often come from legal fees to defend a reporter, not the ultimate settlement or damages. Insurance covers these crucial defense costs.
- The threat environment is changing: The hostility to journalism being whipped up in many places by Trump and his acolytes, especially at the local level, has increased the likelihood of legal challenges to news organizations of all sizes.
- Self-censorship is dangerous: Without adequate insurance protection, newsrooms may avoid important stories that serve the public interest simply because they fear the legal costs of potential challenges.
- Specialized coverage is critical: Just as doctors require specialized policies to cover malpractice claims, journalists need their insurance to cover claims arising from newsgathering and publishing activities. These include not just libel and defamation, but also invasion of privacy, copyright infringement, and newsgathering torts.
About our presenter
Brian Finnell is Vice President of the Main Street Division at EPIC Insurance Brokers & Consultants, where he has specialized in media insurance for over 30 years. Brian Finnell of EPIC has been designated to handle questions and provide help to newsroom applicants for the new AXIS media insurance program. EPIC is a national insurance brokerage with licenses in all 50 states, offering specialized coverage solutions for media organizations.
__________
This webinar is part of the Center for Cooperative Media's ongoing efforts to support local journalism in New Jersey and beyond through practical resources and professional development opportunities.
Questions? Contact the Center for Cooperative Media at info@centerforcooperativemedia.org.

Event title: Statewide News Collective: The community impact of independent journalism
About:
How do you measure the real impact of local journalism on communities?
This webinar will explore innovative approaches to measuring the community impact of local journalism. Drawing on findings from a 2024 study sponsored by the Lenfest Institute for Journalism, it examines the reach and influence of three independent local news organizations — New Hampshire Public Radio, Bridge Michigan, and Montana Free Press.
Using a blend of quantitative and qualitative methods, the study found that audiences of these outlets express high levels of trust in their reporting, are more informed about public affairs, and are more engaged in civic life than other residents. The research also highlights that the impact of local journalism extends across multiple dimensions — from information and trust to participation and community connection.
Our expert presenter will help attendees:
- Understand multiple dimensions of journalism impact assessment
- Learn practical research methods for measuring community engagement
- Discover how to communicate impact findings to funders and partners
- Explore ways to adapt this framework for your own newsroom
This webinar will outline how a range of news organizations can adapt this research framework to assess their own impact and use these insights to communicate their value to funders, partners, and the communities they serve.
This webinar is part of the Collaborative Journalism Resource Hub's Masterclass series.
__________

Event title: Climate resilience and environmental justice for New Jersey communities
About:
Join us on Dec. 9 for a conversation about the innovative steps environmental justice communities are taking to build climate resilience and protect their neighborhoods from extreme weather and flooding.
This webinar will showcase how local organizations are working to make their communities safer and more prepared for climate challenges. You'll hear directly from leaders in New Jersey's environmental justice movement about concrete projects that are helping neighborhoods adapt to climate change, from flood prevention infrastructure to community preparedness programs.
Environmental justice communities are often the most vulnerable to climate impacts, but they're also leading the way in developing creative, community-centered solutions. This conversation will highlight practical approaches to climate resilience that prioritize local needs and build long-term sustainability.
Whether you're a journalist covering climate and environmental issues, a community organizer, or simply interested in climate adaptation strategies, you'll come away with a deeper understanding of how communities are taking action to protect themselves and prepare for the future.

Event agenda
- Welcome and introductions
- Panel discussion with community leaders
- Q&A with attendees

About our speakers/panelists
- New Jersey Environmental Justice Alliance works to advance environmental justice in New Jersey by supporting grassroots organizing, building coalitions, and advocating for policy changes that protect frontline communities from environmental hazards and climate impacts.
- Center for Environmental Transformation (Camden) is a community-based organization that empowers residents to create positive environmental change in Camden through education, advocacy, and hands-on projects that address local environmental challenges.
- Friends of Metcalf Park (East Orange) is a community organization dedicated to preserving and improving Metcalf Park while building community resilience and environmental awareness in East Orange.

Event title: Press briefing: SNAP funding and federal policy changes in New Jersey
About:
Join us on 1/21 for a press briefing on SNAP funding and food insecurity in NJIn New Jersey, more than 800,000 residents rely on the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) benefits. They account for nearly 2 percent of the 42.4 million people in the United States who benefit from the food stamp program. But threats to SNAP, including lapsed funding during the 2025 government shutdown and new federal restrictions, could push more New Jerseyans into food insecurity in the coming years.
A court order compelled the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) to use contingency funds to continue the benefits, but the shutdown underscored how vulnerable federally funded nutrition programs are during political standoffs.
The Trump administration has also threatened to withhold federal SNAP administrative funds from states that refuse to share expanded recipient data, including immigration status. New Jersey is among the states pushing back, arguing that the federal government already has verification access and the new demands risk targeting immigrants and deterring eligible families instead of preventing fraud.

SNAP is a crucial safety net for New Jerseyans:
According to the US Department of Agriculture, more than one million residents, “including 272,000 children (under age 18) and 203,000 seniors (age 60 or older), in New Jersey” are food insecure.8.8% of seniors in New Jersey are food insecure (1 in 11). The number of food insecure seniors in New Jersey increased by 26% year-over-year, almost three times the size of the increase among the general population. (Community Food Bank of New Jersey)About 11.7% of New Jersey residents do not have consistent access to adequate food, a rate close to the national average of 12.2%. (NJDOH)Food insecurity rates by county ranged from 7.3% in Hunterdon County to 14.9% in Hudson County in 2023. (NJDOH)
New SNAP restrictions and policy changes include:
Expanded federal work requirements for Able-Bodied Adults Without Dependents (ABAWDs).Adults between the ages of 18 and 64 who do not qualify for an exemption must complete at least 80 hours per month of employment, approved job training, or qualifying volunteer work to continue receiving benefits beyond three months in a three-year period.Parents and caregivers lose exemption from SNAP work requirements once the youngest child turns 14.
Funding cuts to the SNAP program will disproportionately impact low-wage workers, caregivers, seniors, disabled people, immigrants, and families navigating unstable or seasonal employment. And these changes come as food prices and the cost of living increase, placing more strain on households and local resources such as local food banks and community organizations.
Federal law will cut SNAP spending by $186 billion over the next decade.. Here in NJ, the law creates a $78 million shortfall for county governments and now requires the state budget to raise $100-$300 million to save New Jersey’s SNAP program, according to the NJ Department of Human Services (DHS). Ultimately NJ taxpayers will bear the brunt of the funding shortages.
A DHS statement reads in part, “Programs like NJ FamilyCare and NJ SNAP are not just safety nets. They are lifelines. They reflect New Jersey’s core values of dignity, compassion, and opportunity for all. These programs ensure that children receive medical care, that older adults and people with disabilities can live with independence, and that no family goes hungry in a time of need.”

Join us on Wednesday, January 21, as we bring together food security policy experts, advocates, and local officials and reporters to discuss how New Jersey families are impacted by SNAP policy changes and how communities are responding.

Event title: 2026 Excellence in Community Media Awards
About:
The awards ceremony and luncheon will take place on Friday, March 13, 2026, from 11 am to 2 pm at The College of New Jersey (TCNJ).The Center for Cooperative Media at Montclair State University is pleased to invite you to the 2026 Excellence in Community Media Awards. The awards ceremony and luncheon will take place on Friday, March 13, 2026, from 11 am to 2 pm at The College of New Jersey (TCNJ).
This event is free for members of the NJ News Commons and will bring together journalists, elected officials, representatives from state agencies, academic leaders, and community organizations. It will honor some of the best journalism produced across the state in 2025, with an emphasis on community-level reporting.
What's new this year?
In previous years, we hosted two separate awards: one nominations-based award recognizing outstanding journalists from local and general-market news outlets for specific categories such as engagement, investigative, business, and immigration reporting, and a second honoring top ethnic and community news organizations, including in-language publications, in the state of New Jersey.
This year, the Center adopts a more open approach by consolidating our existing awards into the new Excellence in Community Media Awards, an event celebrating exceptional work from across the NJ News Commons. As part of a new selection process, we’ll select honorees for their impactful, community-driven reporting by drawing on data we’ve collected about hundreds of potential nominations throughout the year via the News Commons. This curator-led approach no longer requires journalists to carve out extra time to identify and submit their work.
“New Jersey is a state where the media landscape is as diverse as its population. For people who live in Atlantic City in the south to Jersey City in the north, local and community media serve as a beacon of trusted journalism, publishing in more than 20 languages,” said Anthony Advincula, director of community media at the Center.
“I am honored to stand alongside the journalists who enrich our lives and communities through their work, despite the significant challenges facing the media today. They are lifelines, dedicated community leaders, and invaluable collaborators, and this recognition reflects our commitment at the Center to ensuring that local media continues to thrive,” said Stefanie Murray, director of the Center.
2026 Excellence in Community Media Awards will recognize NJ journalists and newsrooms in the following categories:
— Excellence in Community Media (up to 10 awards per year)
— Media champion award (1 per year)
— Legacy in journalism award (1 per year)
Some of the nominees have reported in Spanish, Korean, Polish, and English on important issues, including immigration, job opportunities and workplace rights for farmworkers, the senatorial, gubernatorial, and presidential elections, and the voices of the Black community.
These newsrooms have notably demonstrated the press’s role in advocating for justice on issues such as detention, deportation, and the separation of immigrant families, while one of this year’s honorees is helping usher in a new era of New Jersey’s historic Black press. We are proud to recognize their decades-long commitment to informing, educating, and empowering immigrants and communities of color in New Jersey.
______
About the awards
The Excellence in Community News award highlights the significant role that local news organizations play in their communities. It spotlights excellence, impact, and storytelling across all journalism platforms. The award also honors the organizations’ enduring commitment to serving their communities through local journalism.
The Media Champion award recognizes an individual for their vital role as a connector, educator, or local news stakeholder. Each year, the Center will select an honoree for their enduring commitment to serving their communities through local journalism.
The Legacy in Journalism award honors an individual or organization that has made significant contributions to NJ communities and helped to strengthen the state’s local news ecosystem over time. This award recognizes long-term commitment to building community trust and accountability, often carried out across decades and through periods of industry change. Honorees may include journalists, publishers, media organizations, or institutions whose leadership, reporting, mentorship, or advocacy has left a lasting mark on local news in our state.
______
Here are this year’s nominees for the Media Champion and Legacy in Journalism awards:
— Radio Jornalera NJ, an independent bilingual radio station based in Highland Park, NJ, broadcasting in both Spanish and English.— The Korea Daily is the largest Korean publication in New Jersey and New York, based in Palisades Park, NJ. It publishes content in Korean both online and in print.— Front Runner New Jersey is a weekly publication that offers a fresh perspective on the people, events, and issues impacting African Americans and Latinos in South Jersey.— RadioCATA 102.5 FM is a non-commercial community radio station that broadcasts live in Spanish 24/7 from its studio in Bridgeton, NJ. The station’s biweekly program, “Si Se Puede” (Yes We Can), focuses on social justice, immigration rights and policies, workers’ rights, food justice, and community engagement.

Event title: How journalism collaboratives can stay safe
About:
Hear practical, scenario-based guidance from experts to help collaboratives prepare for and respond to evolving safety challenges.Reporting in the field increasingly exposes journalists to complex safety, legal, and digital risks — from covering immigration enforcement operations to documenting protests across U.S. cities. Over the past year, reporters have raised urgent questions about how to navigate encounters with federal agents, distinguish among law enforcement agencies, and protect themselves and their sources from expanding surveillance and on-the-ground threats.
The International Women's Media Foundation (IWMF) will lead a two-part training series. During this one hour session on March 24, the IWMF will provide practical, scenario-based guidance to help reporters prepare for and respond to these evolving challenges. Through real-world examples and actionable safety strategies, this session will equip journalists with the tools to minimize harm, and make informed decisions while covering high-risk assignments.
Following the virtual safety session, the IWMF will lead a half-day training at the Collaborative Journalism Summit on May 14 ahead of the conference kick-off. Participants will learn core skills in risk assessment and mitigation, personal security, covering protests and civil unrest, guidance on PPE, digital safety and a legal know your rights session led by Christina Paia from the Reporter’s Committee for Freedom of the Press.

Register for CJS 2026 here and sign up for the safety training on May 14 here.

Event title: Press briefing: Rising healthcare costs in New Jersey
About:
Join us on Wednesday, 4/22, for a press briefing on the growing healthcare affordability crisis in New Jersey.Healthcare affordability is a growing crisis in New Jersey.More than 727,000 New Jerseyans — roughly 1 in 13 residents — face daily life without health insurance, according to the U.S. Census Bureau. And state advocates also warn that recent federal policy changes such as new Medicaid work requirements and subsidy losses could deepen coverage gaps and financial strain for working families, seniors, disabled residents, and immigrant communities.State data show that per-person healthcare spending in New Jersey rose 6 percent, from $10,663 to $11,319, between 2022 and 2023, while total statewide healthcare spending rose 9.2 %. At the same time, residents have faced higher insurance premiums, growing out-of-pocket costs, and increasing uncertainty about whether they will be able to keep or afford coverage in the years ahead.These pressures are already impacting people’s lives. A New Jersey affordability survey found that:- Nearly 3 in 5 (57%) experienced at least one healthcare affordability burden in the past year.- More than 4 in 5 (85%) worry about affording healthcare in the future.- Lower-income respondents and respondents with disabilities are more likely to go without care and incur debt due to healthcare costs.New Jerseyans are also facing rising premium costs. According to Get Covered New Jersey, the state’s official Affordable Care Act marketplace, many New Jersey residents will face higher health insurance costs in 2026 because enhanced federal Premium Tax Credits expired on December 31, 2025. Those subsidies had helped make coverage more affordable for many enrollees. The Department of Banking and Insurance has also announced that, due to the expiration of those credits and rising medical and pharmacy costs, 2026 individual market rates will increase by an average of 16.6%.In response to rising costs for taxpayers and growing strain on public health programs, Governor Sherrill’s proposed state budget includes reforms to the State Health Benefits Program. The plan also calls for $145 million in fines on large employers such as Amazon, Target, and Walmart, whose workers rely on Medicaid, with charges of up to $725 annually per covered worker to help offset state Medicaid costs.

Join us on Wednesday, April 22, for a conversation about the factors driving healthcare costs in New Jersey, how those costs are affecting families and communities, and what story angles reporters should be watching across insurance, hospital pricing, medical debt, and access to care. We’ll hear from policy experts, advocates, and academics about current impact and how state and federal decisions are shaping what comes next.\\\\\\\\\\\📅 Date: Wednesday, April 22, 2026🕒 Time: 2:00 PM - 3:00 PM EDT📍 Location: Virtual (Zoom link will be provided after registration)\\\\\\\\\\\## Panelists to be announced\\\\\\\\\\\This event is part of our "Not Your Same Old Sources" (NYSOS) series, featuring experts from our Diverse Sources Database. It's presented in partnership with NJ.com and NJ ethnic and community media outlets.As a reminder, University events are open to everyone.
